posts.title: GPT-5.6: OpenAI Ships Three Models, One Family
posts.subtitle: Sol, Terra, and Luna trade price for speed across a shared 1.05M-token context window.
posts.summary: GPT-5.6 replaced OpenAI's single flagship with three priced tiers — Sol, Terra, Luna — sharing one 1.05M-token context and full tool suite, with Sol posting its clearest wins on agentic benchmarks like Terminal-Bench and OSWorld.

slides.body: On July 9, 2026, GPT-5.6 shipped to general availability across ChatGPT, Codex, and the API as three distinct models instead of one: Sol, Terra, and Luna.
slides.title: OpenAI Retires a Single Flagship for a Three-Tier Family

bullets: A single frontier model forces every request — a one-line autocomplete and a multi-hour agent run — through the same cost and latency.
OpenAI's fix: split by capability tier instead of shipping one 'best' model.
Sol is the flagship reasoning/agentic ceiling, Terra is balanced everyday work, Luna is the cheapest and fastest tier.
slides.title: One Model Was Never the Right Answer for Every Call

bullets: Every tier ships the same tool surface: web_search, file_search, image_generation, code_interpreter, hosted_shell, apply_patch, skills, computer_use, mcp, tool_search.
Reasoning effort is selectable per call, letting Terra or Luna trade latency for accuracy on demand.
slides.body: All three tiers share a 1.05M-token context window, a 128K-token max output, and a February 16, 2026 knowledge cutoff — the difference is reasoning depth and price, not capability surface.
slides.title: Same Skeleton, Three Price Points

bullets: Sol's clean wins land specifically on agentic benchmarks: Terminal-Bench, BrowseComp, OSWorld, and Agents' Last Exam.
slides.title: Terminal-Bench: Sol Beats Claude Opus 4.8, Trails Claude Mythos 5

bullets: Cross 272K input tokens in one request and pricing jumps to 2x input / 1.5x output for the whole call — Luna's long-context rate becomes $2/$9, Sol's becomes $10/$45.
That cliff makes prompt trimming a real cost lever, not just a latency one, once you're past roughly a quarter-million input tokens.
slides.title: Pricing Scales With the Reasoning You Ask For

bullets: All three tiers share the same February 16, 2026 training cutoff — five months old at launch — so recency-sensitive tasks still need retrieval or the web_search tool, not the model's parametric memory.

Splitting into three tiers means benchmarking your own workload before defaulting to Sol: Terra beats Luna by only 1.8 points on Terminal-Bench (84.3% vs 82.5%) for roughly 2.5x the price — not always worth it.
slides.title: The Catch: A Five-Month-Stale Knowledge Cutoff

bullets: Rule of thumb from the pricing table: Luna is roughly 5x cheaper than Sol on both input and output — worth it only if Sol's accuracy gain actually changes the outcome.
slides.body: A support-ticket triage agent classifying and routing 50,000 tickets a day doesn't need Sol's ceiling — Luna's $1/$6 pricing and shared tool access (including computer_use) make it the default, reserving Sol for the subset of tickets that need multi-step investigation.
slides.title: Picking a Tier for a Real Workload

bullets: GPT-5.6 replaces a single flagship with three tiers (Sol/Terra/Luna) sharing one 1.05M-token context and full tool suite.
Sol leads specifically on agentic/tool-use benchmarks (Terminal-Bench, BrowseComp, OSWorld) — not necessarily general knowledge tasks.
A long-context pricing cliff kicks in at 272K input tokens, applying equally across all three tiers.
Default to the cheapest tier that clears your accuracy bar; upgrade only the workloads that actually need it.
slides.title: Takeaways

questions.prompt: How many models make up the GPT-5.6 family?
options: Two
Three
Four
Five
questions.explanation: GPT-5.6 shipped as three tiers — Sol, Terra, and Luna — sharing the same context window and tools but differing in reasoning depth and price.

questions.prompt: What happens to GPT-5.6 pricing once a request exceeds 272K input tokens?
options: Nothing, price stays flat
Input price doubles and output price rises 1.5x for the whole request
Only output tokens get more expensive
The request is rejected
questions.explanation: Long-context requests over 272K input tokens are billed at 2x the input rate and 1.5x the output rate for the entire call, across all three tiers — a real reason to trim context before crossing that line.

questions.prompt: On which category of benchmark does Sol post its clearest wins over GPT-5.5 and Claude's models?
options: General trivia knowledge
Agentic/tool-use benchmarks like Terminal-Bench and OSWorld
Image generation quality
Translation accuracy
questions.explanation: Sol's standout results are concentrated in agentic evaluations — Terminal-Bench, BrowseComp, OSWorld, and Agents' Last Exam — reflecting an emphasis on tool use and multi-step task execution rather than raw knowledge recall.

posts.title: Byte Pair Encoding: Text Into Numbers
posts.subtitle: Why 'unbelievable' is one token and a rare word can cost five.
posts.summary: Tokenizers split text into subword chunks using Byte Pair Encoding, a bottom-up merge algorithm — and that split, not word count, is what actually drives an LLM's cost, context limits, and quirks like letter-counting failures.

slides.body: Before a transformer sees a single word, a tokenizer has already broken your text into subword chunks called tokens — and that split shapes cost, context limits, and even math mistakes.
slides.title: Every LLM Reads Tokens, Never Words

bullets: Word-level vocabularies explode: English alone needs hundreds of thousands of entries, and any typo or new word becomes an 'unknown' token the model has never seen.
Character-level vocabularies solve coverage but make sequences 4-5x longer — costly for a transformer whose compute grows quadratically with sequence length.
Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) sits between the two: a fixed vocabulary of whole words, common word pieces, and individual bytes as a fallback.
slides.title: Why Not Just Split on Whitespace?

bullets: Example: if 'e' and 's' co-occur constantly, they merge into 'es'; if 'es' and 't' then co-occur often, they merge into 'est' — common suffixes end up as single tokens.
Because it starts from bytes, BPE can always fall back to encoding any Unicode character, even ones it has never seen — nothing is truly 'unknown'.
slides.body: Training starts from raw bytes and repeatedly merges the most frequent adjacent pair into a new token, growing the vocabulary from the bottom up until it hits a target size.
slides.title: BPE Builds Its Vocabulary by Merging, Not Splitting

bullets: Doubling the vocabulary in o200k_base means more whole-word and multi-character tokens, so the same English sentence encodes into fewer tokens — the biggest gains show up on non-English text and code.
Fewer tokens for the same text lowers real cost even at an identical price-per-token, since API pricing is metered per token, not per character.
slides.title: Vocabulary Size Changed When OpenAI Shipped GPT-4o

bullets: Numbers show the same issue: '380' might tokenize as one chunk while '381' splits differently, which is part of why LLMs are unreliable at multi-digit arithmetic without a tool call.
slides.body: Ask a model to count the letters in 'strawberry' and it often gets it wrong — because the model never sees the letters s-t-r-a-w-b-e-r-r-y. It sees two or three opaque token IDs, and has to infer spelling from patterns in training data rather than reading characters directly.
slides.title: Why 'strawberry' Trips Up Letter-Counting

bullets: Non-English languages tokenize far less efficiently than English — a sentence that's 20 tokens in English can be 40-60 tokens in Hindi or Thai on the same tokenizer, directly inflating cost and eating into context limits for non-English users.
Tokenizer boundaries are also a known filter-bypass surface: splitting a blocked word across an unusual token boundary can sometimes slip past keyword-based filters that operate on decoded text.
You can't mix tokenizers across model families — a prompt tuned for one model's token boundaries doesn't transfer its token count or behavior to another.
slides.title: The Pitfalls: Cost, Language Bias, and Filter Bypasses

slides.body: Most teams estimate cost by running the exact tokenizer library (like OpenAI's tiktoken) locally before a call, rather than guessing from word count. A rough rule of thumb is ~4 characters per token for English text — but that ratio breaks down for code, non-English text, and made-up words.
slides.title: Counting Tokens Before You Send the Request

bullets: Tokenizers split text into subword chunks via BPE — trained by repeatedly merging the most frequent adjacent byte/character pairs.
Bigger vocabularies (o200k_base's ~200K vs cl100k_base's ~100K) mean fewer tokens per sentence and lower real-world cost.
Models don't see letters — they see token IDs, which explains character-counting and digit-arithmetic failures.
Non-English text tokenizes less efficiently, a real and often-overlooked cost and context-budget penalty.
slides.title: Takeaways

questions.prompt: What does Byte Pair Encoding do during training?
options: Splits text only on whitespace
Repeatedly merges the most frequent adjacent pair of bytes/characters into new tokens
Translates text into a fixed set of 26 letter tokens
Removes all punctuation before tokenizing
questions.explanation: BPE builds its vocabulary bottom-up: starting from raw bytes, it repeatedly merges the most frequent adjacent pair into a single new token until it reaches a target vocabulary size.

questions.prompt: Why do LLMs often miscount letters in a word like 'strawberry'?
options: They are not trained on English
The model sees token IDs, not individual letters, so spelling must be inferred rather than read directly
Tokenizers remove vowels
The model has a maximum word length of 5 letters
questions.explanation: A word like 'strawberry' is usually encoded as two or three opaque token IDs. The model never directly observes the letter sequence, so exact character counting requires inferring spelling from training patterns instead of reading characters — a task tokenized models are inherently bad at.

questions.prompt: What's a real-world cost consequence of a larger tokenizer vocabulary like o200k_base (~200K) versus cl100k_base (~100K)?
options: Larger vocabularies always cost more per API call
The same text encodes into fewer tokens, which can lower cost even at the same price-per-token
Larger vocabularies only affect image models
Vocabulary size has no effect on tokens produced
questions.explanation: A bigger vocabulary can represent more text as single tokens (whole words, common multi-character sequences), so the same sentence — especially non-English text or code — needs fewer tokens overall, which reduces cost since API pricing is metered per token.

references.title: Neural Machine Translation of Rare Words with Subword Units (BPE)
tiktoken — OpenAI's fast BPE tokenizer

posts.title: Agent Memory: Giving LLM Agents a Past Beyond the Context Window
posts.subtitle: Context windows reset every session. Memory systems are how an agent keeps knowing you after the tokens are gone.
posts.summary: A context window is a scratchpad that vanishes at the end of a session — it is not memory. Real agent memory is a separate subsystem with its own write, consolidation, retrieval, and eviction policies, split into working, episodic, and semantic stores, that lets an agent stay consistent with a user across sessions it shares no tokens with.

slides.title: Every New Session, Your Agent Meets You for the First Time
slides.body: A context window is not memory — it's a scratchpad that gets wiped the moment a session ends or the token budget runs out. Agent memory is the separate system that lets facts, preferences, and past decisions survive past that boundary.
bullets: Frameworks like MemGPT and Letta treat memory as a first-class subsystem, not just a longer context window
Consumer products (ChatGPT memory, Claude projects) are productized versions of the same idea
The core trick: decide what's worth writing down, then retrieve only what's relevant later

slides.title: A Bigger Context Window Doesn't Give You Memory
slides.body: It's tempting to think 'just use a huge context window and stuff everything in.' That fails in practice for three separate reasons.
bullets: Cost and latency: attention cost grows with context length, so replaying a full history every call gets slower and more expensive as it grows
Context rot: models attend unevenly across very long inputs, so facts buried in the middle of a huge transcript can get effectively ignored
No persistence: once a session ends or the app restarts, the context window is gone — there's nothing to reload without an explicit save step

slides.title: Three Kinds of Memory an Agent Actually Needs
slides.body: Borrowing loosely from cognitive science, agent memory is usually split into stores with different write and read patterns.
bullets: Working memory: the live scratchpad for the current task — the context window itself
Episodic memory: a log of specific past events ('user rejected this API design on the March review') retrieved by similarity when relevant
Semantic memory: distilled, durable facts and preferences ('user prefers pytest over unittest') consolidated out of episodes over time

slides.title: The Memory Pipeline: Write, Consolidate, Retrieve, Forget
slides.body: A working memory system is four policies wired together, not just 'a vector database.'
bullets: Write policy: what's worth saving? Typically a scoring pass judges each turn for salience before anything is persisted
Consolidation: periodically summarize clusters of episodic entries into compact semantic facts, so retrieval isn't sifting raw transcripts forever
Retrieval: embed the current query, run a similarity search over stored memories, and inject only the top-k relevant hits back into the prompt
Eviction: stale, low-salience, or contradicted memories get pruned or overwritten — skip this and memory quietly accumulates conflicting facts

slides.title: Worked Example: A Coding Agent Across Five Sessions
slides.body: Session 1: the user tells the agent 'always write tests with pytest, never unittest.' Without memory, this instruction is gone the moment the session ends.
bullets: With memory, that instruction is written as a semantic fact once session 1 ends
In session 5 — a different task, days later — the agent's retrieval step pulls that fact back in before it writes any test code, unprompted
The user never repeats the preference; the agent's behavior stays consistent across sessions that share no context window at all

slides.title: Three Ways to Give an Agent a Long-Term Memory

rows: Token cost and latency grow directly with history length
Detail loss — summaries can drop specifics the agent later needs
Embedded chunks of past episodes and facts
Retrieval is lossy and can miss memories the embedding didn't rank highly

slides.title: Where Agent Memory Goes Wrong
slides.body: Memory adds a whole new failure surface, not just a nice-to-have feature.
bullets: Stale or contradicted facts: if the user changes their mind, an old memory can silently override the new instruction unless the write policy checks for conflicts
Latency: every turn now costs an extra retrieval round-trip against the memory store, on top of the LLM call itself
Memory poisoning: a malicious or buggy tool output written into long-term memory persists across sessions — a far longer-lived attack surface than a single-session prompt injection
Skip elaborate memory for short, single-session tasks — the added complexity and latency isn't worth it if the context window comfortably holds the whole task anyway

slides.title: Memory Is a Retrieval System, Not a Bigger Prompt
slides.body: Treat agent memory as its own subsystem with explicit write, consolidation, retrieval, and eviction policies — not as an afterthought bolted onto the context window.
bullets: Working memory (the context window) handles the current task; episodic and semantic memory handle everything before it
Consolidation keeps retrieval fast by turning raw episodes into compact, durable facts
Always design an eviction policy — memory that only ever grows eventually surfaces contradictory or irrelevant results
Best for: assistants and agents used repeatedly by the same user or team over many sessions; skip it for one-off, single-session tasks

questions.prompt: What's the main difference between an agent's context window and its long-term memory system?
options: There is no difference — they're the same mechanism
The context window is temporary working memory for the current session; long-term memory persists facts across sessions
Long-term memory is just a larger context window
Context windows are only used by chatbots, not agents
questions.explanation: The context window resets when a session ends or fills up. A separate memory system (episodic and semantic stores) is what lets facts survive past that boundary.

questions.prompt: Why do agent memory systems need a consolidation step, not just raw episodic logging?
options: To encrypt sensitive memories
To compress the model's weights
To turn scattered raw events into compact, distilled facts so retrieval stays fast and relevant over time
Consolidation is only needed for image-based memories
questions.explanation: Without consolidation, episodic memory just keeps growing, and retrieval has to sift through more raw transcripts over time. Summarizing clusters of episodes into stable facts keeps retrieval fast and precise.

questions.prompt: What's a key risk that agent memory introduces which a stateless single-session agent doesn't have?
options: Higher GPU training costs
Memory poisoning — a bad tool output or injected instruction can get written into long-term memory and affect every future session
The agent can no longer use tools
The context window becomes permanently fixed
questions.explanation: A stateless agent's mistakes disappear when the session ends. A persistent memory store carries a corrupted or malicious entry forward into every future session until it's found and evicted.

posts.title: Chunking Strategies: Where You Split Decides What You Retrieve
posts.subtitle: Fixed-size splitting cuts sentences in half — semantic and structure-aware chunking cut where meaning actually breaks
posts.summary: Every RAG pipeline embeds chunks, not whole documents — and the chunking method decides whether a retrieved passage actually contains a complete, self-sufficient answer. Fixed-size splitting is fast but blind to structure; recursive and semantic chunking split at real boundaries; parent-document retrieval decouples the unit you search from the unit you feed the model.

slides.title: The retrieval step you never see is deciding your answer quality
slides.body: Before a single embedding is computed, every RAG pipeline makes an invisible decision: how to cut source documents into chunks. Get it wrong and the best retriever and the best LLM in the world can't save you — they're working from a fragment that's missing the sentence that mattered.
bullets: RAG never embeds a whole document — it embeds chunks
Chunk boundaries determine whether a retrieved passage is self-contained
The same retriever, given differently-chunked input, returns different answers

slides.title: Fixed-size chunking is blind to structure
slides.body: The simplest approach — slice every N tokens (say 512), regardless of what's there — is fast and popular, but it doesn't know where a sentence, a table row, or a numbered step ends. It will happily cut a definition in half, split a table's header row from its data, or sever a caveat from the rule it qualifies.
bullets: A 512-token cut can land mid-sentence, mid-table, or mid-code-block
The second half of a split sentence embeds to a vector that means almost nothing alone
Retrieval then returns a fragment that answers half the question, or none of it

slides.title: Three ways to split at real boundaries
slides.body: Recursive character splitting tries a list of separators in priority order (double-newline, then newline, then sentence, then word) so it prefers to break at a paragraph boundary and only falls back to a mid-sentence cut when a paragraph itself exceeds the size limit. Structure-aware splitting uses the document's own markup (Markdown headers, HTML tags, code fences) to keep each section, list, or function intact. Semantic chunking skips fixed sizes entirely.
bullets: Recursive splitting: try paragraph → sentence → word, in that priority order
Structure-aware: split on headers/sections/code-fences, not token counts
Semantic chunking: split where the *meaning* changes, not where the counter hits a limit

slides.title: How semantic chunking finds a breakpoint
slides.body: Split the text into sentences, embed each one, then slide along the sequence comparing the cosine similarity between consecutive sentence embeddings. A sharp drop in similarity signals a topic shift — that's where you cut. A common rule: flag any drop below a percentile threshold (e.g. the bottom 10% of similarity scores seen so far) as a breakpoint, then group everything between breakpoints into one chunk.
bullets: Embed sentence i and sentence i+1, compute cosine similarity between them
A similarity drop past a percentile threshold marks a topic boundary
Chunks end up variable-length — a focused paragraph stays whole, a topic-switching page gets cut

slides.title: Parent-document retrieval: search small, return big
slides.body: Small chunks embed more precisely (less topic dilution per vector) but lack surrounding context when handed to the LLM. Parent-document retrieval resolves the tension by decoupling the two: index small child chunks (e.g. 100-200 tokens) for search, but each child stores a pointer to its larger parent section (e.g. 1500 tokens). A query matches the precise child, then the pipeline swaps in the parent before generation.
bullets: Index unit (child, ~100-200 tokens) ≠ context unit (parent, ~1500 tokens)
Search precision comes from the small child embedding
Answer completeness comes from the larger parent that gets fed to the LLM

slides.title: Picking a strategy

rows: Lowest — no embedding calls to chunk
Still size-capped; can split a long paragraph
Low-medium — needs a parser per format
Fails on messy or unstructured source
Highest — embeds every sentence to chunk
Dense technical text with abrupt topic shifts
Slow and costly to build the index
Long sections where child chunks lose context
More storage; extra lookup step at query time

slides.title: One clause, two outcomes
slides.body: A 40-page vendor contract has this sentence spanning a chunk boundary under fixed 256-token splitting: '...the Customer may terminate this Agreement for convenience' | 'provided that at least 90 days’ written notice is given.' The retriever surfaces only the first half for the query 'can we cancel anytime?' — the answer looks like an unconditional yes. A recursive splitter, preferring to break at the paragraph before this sentence, keeps the full clause with its 90-day condition intact, and the retrieved chunk answers correctly.
bullets: Query: "can we cancel anytime?"
Fixed-size chunk: only sees "...may terminate for convenience" — misleading
Recursively-split chunk: keeps the full clause including the 90-day notice condition

slides.title: Tradeoffs and when to skip the fancy stuff
slides.body: Chunk size itself is a tradeoff independent of strategy: too small and each chunk loses the surrounding context needed to disambiguate it; too large and irrelevant sentences dilute the embedding, pulling it away from the query's true topic and adding noise the LLM has to filter out. Semantic chunking's extra embedding pass adds real latency and cost to ingestion — for a small, uniformly-formatted corpus (FAQs, short articles) that cost rarely pays for itself over plain recursive splitting.
bullets: Too small: loses context, forces heavy reliance on overlap or parent lookups
Too large: dilutes the embedding and pads the LLM's context with irrelevant text
Semantic chunking's ingestion cost isn't worth it for small or uniformly short documents
Overlap (10-20% of chunk size) is cheap insurance against split-boundary loss for any strategy

slides.title: Chunk for how the text is actually shaped
bullets: Fixed-size chunking is fast but blind — it cuts sentences, tables, and clauses mid-way
Recursive and structure-aware splitting prefer real boundaries (paragraphs, headers, code fences) before falling back to a hard size limit
Semantic chunking cuts where sentence-embedding similarity drops — worth the extra cost for dense, topic-shifting text
Parent-document retrieval separates the precise search unit (small child) from the context-rich generation unit (larger parent)
Chunk size is its own tradeoff: too small loses context, too large dilutes relevance — a 10-20% overlap is cheap insurance either way

questions.prompt: Why can fixed-size token chunking hurt retrieval quality even though it's the simplest method?
options: It's too slow to run on large document sets
It cuts purely on a token count, so it can split a sentence, table, or clause in half regardless of meaning, producing fragments that embed to a vague or misleading vector
It only works with English-language documents
It requires a GPU to compute chunk boundaries
questions.explanation: Fixed-size chunking has no awareness of sentence, paragraph, or table structure — it just counts tokens. A cut landing mid-clause produces a fragment whose embedding no longer represents a complete idea, which is exactly what happened to the termination clause in the contract example.

questions.prompt: In parent-document retrieval, why index the small child chunk instead of just indexing the large parent section directly?
options: Small chunks are cheaper to store than large ones
A smaller chunk produces a more precise, less diluted embedding for matching the query, while the larger parent (swapped in after the match) supplies the surrounding context the LLM needs
Large sections cannot be embedded at all due to token limits
It removes the need for a vector database entirely
questions.explanation: A large chunk's embedding averages together many sentences on possibly different subtopics, diluting precision. Parent-document retrieval gets the best of both: the child's focused embedding for accurate matching, and the parent's full context for generation.

questions.prompt: When is semantic (embedding-based) chunking most likely NOT worth its extra cost?
options: When documents are long, technical, and shift topics abruptly within a page
When the corpus is small and made up of short, uniformly-structured documents like FAQs
When retrieval precision matters more than ingestion speed
When the source text has no clear paragraph structure at all
questions.explanation: Semantic chunking requires embedding every sentence just to decide where to cut, which adds real cost at ingestion time. For a small, uniformly short corpus, plain recursive splitting gets nearly the same result for a fraction of the cost — the extra precision has little room to matter.

posts.title: Agents-A1: A 35B Model Trained to Match Trillion-Parameter Rivals
posts.subtitle: Instead of adding parameters, this agent scales the length and diversity of what it practices on.
posts.summary: Agents-A1 is a 35B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts agent that matches or beats trillion-parameter models like Kimi-K2.6 and DeepSeek-V4-pro on agentic benchmarks — not by growing bigger, but by training on much longer, more varied agent trajectories through a three-stage multi-teacher distillation recipe.

slides.title: 35B Parameters, Trillion-Parameter Results
bullets: Paper: "Scaling the Horizon, Not the Parameters" (arXiv:2606.30616)
Agents-A1: a 35B Mixture-of-Experts agentic model
Matches trillion-parameter models like Kimi-K2.6 and DeepSeek-V4-pro on agent benchmarks

slides.title: The Industry's Default Lever Is Just 'Bigger'
slides.body: Frontier agentic models have mostly chased capability by scaling parameter count into the trillions. That buys performance, but it also buys enormous inference cost — every token generated by a 1T-parameter model activates far more compute than a 35B one, even with Mixture-of-Experts routing.
bullets: Trillion-parameter agents (Kimi-K2.6, DeepSeek-V4-pro) are expensive to serve at scale
Bigger models don't automatically learn to plan, use tools, or survive long tasks better
The paper asks: what if you scaled the agent's *practice*, not its *size*?

slides.title: Scale the Horizon: Longer Tasks, More Domains
slides.body: Agents-A1 builds a long-horizon knowledge-action infrastructure connecting external knowledge, actions, observations, and verifier outcomes — producing training trajectories that average 45,000 tokens, far longer than typical single-turn fine-tuning examples.
bullets: Stage 1: full-domain supervised fine-tuning for broad agentic behavior
Stage 2: train separate domain-level teacher models, each an expert in one domain
Stage 3: multi-teacher, domain-routed on-policy distillation with vocabulary alignment — merges the teachers' expertise back into one 35B student
Trajectories average ~45K tokens, spanning six distinct agentic domains

slides.title: A 35B Model Trading Blows With 1T Models

rows: Best score among all models tested, including 1T-class
Best overall in the paper's evaluation suite
Best overall, ahead of larger frontier models

slides.title: Same Question, Two Very Different Training Diets
slides.body: Imagine two 35B models given the same coding-agent task: fix a multi-file bug, running tests, reading logs, adjusting a plan when the first fix fails. A model trained only on short, single-step examples has never practiced holding a 40-step plan together. Agents-A1's 45K-token trajectories mean it has already seen thousands of long, messy, multi-step tasks where the first attempt failed and the plan had to adapt — so a novel long task looks like more of the same, not something unfamiliar.
bullets: Long trajectories teach the model to recover from failed sub-steps, not just execute cleanly
Domain-routed distillation means a 'coding' teacher and a 'research' teacher both shape the same student
The student model ends up generalizing across domains its teachers never saw combined

slides.title: Where Horizon-Scaling Has Real Costs
slides.body: This approach doesn't remove cost — it moves it. Building the long-horizon knowledge-action infrastructure and training a dedicated teacher model per domain is expensive engineering before the student ever sees a single trajectory.
bullets: Requires building verifier infrastructure for every domain you want to distill from
Six domain-teacher models must each be trained and validated before distillation starts
Vocabulary alignment across teachers adds a non-trivial extra training stage
Performance outside the six trained domains is untested — this is not a general 'any-task' guarantee

slides.title: Parameters Aren't the Only Scaling Axis
bullets: A 35B agent can match 1T-class models by training on longer, harder, more varied trajectories
Three-stage recipe: broad SFT → domain teachers → multi-teacher on-policy distillation
45K-token average trajectory length is the core lever, not extra parameters
The tradeoff: cheaper inference, but expensive, domain-specific training infrastructure upfront

questions.prompt: What is the core idea behind Agents-A1's 'scaling the horizon' approach?
options: Adding more parameters until it matches 1T-class models
Training on longer, more varied agentic trajectories instead of growing model size
Using a bigger vocabulary for tokenization
Running the model for more inference steps at test time
questions.explanation: Agents-A1 stays at 35B parameters but trains on trajectories averaging 45K tokens across six domains, arguing that the length and diversity of practice matters as much as raw model size.

questions.prompt: What does Stage 3 of the training recipe (multi-teacher domain-routed on-policy distillation) actually do?
options: Deletes the domain teachers and retrains from scratch
Merges the specialized knowledge of separate domain-teacher models into one student model
Increases the student model's parameter count to match the teachers
Removes agentic capabilities and adds general chat ability
questions.explanation: Stage 3 distills knowledge from multiple domain-specific teacher models into the single 35B student, using vocabulary alignment so knowledge transfers cleanly across domains.

questions.prompt: What's a real limitation of the horizon-scaling approach described in the paper?
options: It only works on GPUs with less than 8GB of memory
It requires no training infrastructure at all
Building domain-teacher models and verifier infrastructure for each domain is expensive, and performance outside those domains is unproven
It cannot use Mixture-of-Experts architectures
questions.explanation: The approach trades cheaper inference for expensive, domain-specific upfront training infrastructure — and the paper's results are demonstrated only within the six domains it was trained and evaluated on.

references.title: Scaling the Horizon, Not the Parameters: Reaching Trillion-Parameter Performance with a 35B Agent (arXiv:2606.30616)

posts.title: Muse Spark 1.1: Meta's First Paid Agentic Model
posts.subtitle: Meta Superintelligence Labs opens a public API where the model plans, delegates to subagents, and decides when to click versus script.
posts.summary: On July 9, 2026, Meta Superintelligence Labs released Muse Spark 1.1 and opened a public preview of the Meta Model API — Meta's first paid, agent-native model, built to orchestrate multi-agent workflows across native tools, MCP servers, and computer-use tasks.

slides.title: Meta Starts Charging for Agentic AI
bullets: Muse Spark 1.1 — released July 9, 2026 by Meta Superintelligence Labs
Public preview of the Meta Model API, Meta's first paid model API
Built for multi-agent orchestration, tool use, and computer use

slides.title: Meta Was a Chat Model in an Agent-Model World
slides.body: While OpenAI, Anthropic, and xAI shipped models designed to plan, call tools, and run for long stretches unsupervised, Meta's prior open releases were largely optimized for chat and generation rather than agentic orchestration — leaving it without a serious answer in the agent-API market.
bullets: Competitors already had agent-native APIs (Claude, GPT-5.x, Grok) with paid tiers
Meta's earlier Llama releases weren't tuned for multi-step tool orchestration
No prior Meta model API charged for agentic workloads at all

slides.title: A Model That Plans, Delegates, and Chooses Its Method
slides.body: Muse Spark 1.1 is trained to act as either a main agent that plans and delegates work, or a subagent that executes a narrow job and escalates when stuck. It zero-shot generalizes to tools it wasn't explicitly trained on, including new MCP servers and custom skills.
bullets: Can act as orchestrator (planning, delegating) or as a subagent (executing, escalating)
Zero-shot generalizes to new native tools, MCP servers, and custom skills
Actively manages a 1M-token context window across long sessions
Chooses per-step whether to write a script, click through a UI, or batch multiple actions at once

slides.title: What's Actually New Versus Prior Meta Releases

rows: $1.25 in / $4.25 out per million tokens, plus $20 free credit
1M tokens, actively managed rather than just extended
Drop-in replacement for OpenAI and Anthropic SDK calling conventions
Competitive with Claude Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5 on tool-use tasks, trailing on some coding and computer-use benchmarks

slides.title: One Task, Split Across an Orchestrator and Subagents
slides.body: Picture asking Muse Spark 1.1 to reorganize a shared drive and update a spreadsheet from the results. As the main agent, it plans the steps, then spins up a subagent to handle file operations (choosing to script the bulk renames instead of clicking each file) while it directly clicks through the spreadsheet UI itself, since a script isn't worth writing for a handful of cell edits.
bullets: Main agent decomposes the task and hands off a bounded subtask to a subagent
Subagent decides scripting is faster for repetitive file operations
Main agent decides direct UI interaction is faster for a small, one-off edit
Escalation path exists if the subagent gets stuck partway through

slides.title: It's a Preview, Not a Finished Product
slides.body: Muse Spark 1.1 is Meta's first attempt at a paid agentic API, and it shows in the fine print: it's US-only, it's a preview (not a stable GA release), and independent evaluations place it behind Claude Opus 4.8 on computer-use and coding benchmarks like OSWorld and SWE-Bench Pro.
bullets: US-only public preview — not yet globally available
Trails Claude Opus 4.8 on computer-use and some coding benchmarks
Meta has no track record yet running a paid, SLA-backed agent API at scale
Zero-shot tool generalization is a strong claim that will need broader real-world testing

slides.title: Meta's Agent Play, In Three Points
bullets: Muse Spark 1.1 is Meta's first paid, agent-native model API — a real shift from chat-only releases
Its distinguishing feature is orchestration: deciding when to delegate, script, or click
It's competitive on tool-use benchmarks but still behind frontier rivals on coding and computer-use
Worth watching as Meta's on-ramp into the same agent-API market as OpenAI, Anthropic, and xAI

questions.prompt: What is significant about Muse Spark 1.1's launch for Meta specifically?
options: It's Meta's first open-source model
It's Meta's first paid, agent-native model API
It's Meta's first image generation model
It replaces all of Meta's chat products
questions.explanation: Prior Meta model releases (like Llama) were free and chat-oriented; Muse Spark 1.1 is Meta's first paid API built specifically for agentic orchestration.

questions.prompt: What does it mean that Muse Spark 1.1 can act as either a 'main agent' or a 'subagent'?
options: It can run two copies of itself on different servers
It can either plan and delegate work, or execute a narrow task and escalate when stuck
It switches between English and other languages
It alternates between chat mode and image mode
questions.explanation: As a main agent it plans and delegates; as a subagent it sticks to a bounded job and escalates back up if it can't complete it — enabling multi-agent orchestration.

questions.prompt: Based on early benchmarks, where does Muse Spark 1.1 currently fall short of rivals like Claude Opus 4.8?
options: It has no context window at all
It cannot call any external tools
It trails on some coding and computer-use benchmarks like SWE-Bench Pro and OSWorld
It's only available as a paid model with no free tier
questions.explanation: Independent evaluations show Muse Spark 1.1 competitive on tool-use tasks but behind Claude Opus 4.8 on some coding and computer-use benchmarks — a real gap for a first-generation agentic release.

posts.title: Cross-Encoder Reranking: The Second Pass That Fixes Vector Search
posts.subtitle: Vector search finds documents that look related. Reranking checks whether they actually answer the question.
posts.summary: Bi-encoder vector search retrieves candidates fast by comparing precomputed embeddings, but it can't model fine-grained query-document interaction. Cross-encoder reranking runs a second, more expensive pass over just the top candidates, jointly attending over the query and each document to fix the retrieval mistakes vector search alone can't catch.

slides.title: Retrieval Gets You Close. Reranking Gets You Right.
bullets: A second-stage step in RAG pipelines, run after initial vector/keyword retrieval
Fixes cases where 'semantically similar' isn't the same as 'actually relevant'
Standard technique behind most production-grade RAG and search systems

slides.title: Bi-Encoders Score Documents Without Ever Comparing Them
slides.body: Vector search embeds the query and every document separately, then ranks by cosine similarity between those fixed vectors. Each document's embedding was computed with zero knowledge of the specific query — so two documents that are topically similar but differ in the one detail that matters can end up scored almost identically.
bullets: Query and document embeddings are computed completely independently
A document can score high on general topic overlap while missing the specific answer
No token-to-token interaction between query and document is possible in this scoring step

slides.title: Cross-Encoders Read Query and Document Together
slides.body: A cross-encoder concatenates the query and a candidate document into a single input, runs it through a transformer, and outputs one relevance score. Because query tokens and document tokens attend to each other directly, it can catch fine-grained relevance signals a bi-encoder's separate embeddings miss.
bullets: Input: [query, document] pair, jointly encoded — not two separate embeddings
Full self-attention lets query tokens directly attend to document tokens
Output: one relevance score per pair, far more accurate than cosine similarity
Too expensive to run over an entire corpus — only viable on a small candidate set

slides.title: Two Stages, Two Very Different Jobs

rows: Slow — one forward pass per candidate
Tens to a few hundred candidates

slides.title: When 'Related' Beats a Reranker That Reads Closely
slides.body: A user asks: "What's the max upload file size?" Vector search returns three chunks, all about storage limits and file handling, ranked by topical similarity — but the chunk with the actual number ("250MB per file") ranks third because its surrounding text is sparse and less semantically rich than the other two. A cross-encoder reranker, reading the query against each chunk jointly, recognizes that only one chunk directly answers the question and promotes it to first.
bullets: Vector search's top result can be topically on-target but factually unhelpful
The reranker jointly scores query and chunk, not just embedding similarity
Typical pipeline: retrieve top 50-100 by vector search, rerank down to top 5-10

slides.title: When Reranking Isn't Worth It
slides.body: Reranking adds real latency and cost — it's a per-candidate model inference, not a lookup. Applying it in the wrong place either wastes compute or hides a deeper retrieval problem.
bullets: Don't rerank huge candidate sets — cost scales linearly with candidates, so bound it to ~50-100
A reranker can't rescue a first-stage retrieval that missed the right document entirely
Adds a network hop and inference latency — often 50-200ms extra per query
Needs its own evaluation; a reranker fine-tuned on the wrong domain can hurt more than help

slides.title: Two-Stage Retrieval Is the Default for a Reason
bullets: Bi-encoders retrieve cheaply at scale; cross-encoders rerank a short list accurately
The gain comes from joint query-document attention, which embeddings alone can't provide
Always bound the reranked set (~50-100 candidates) to control latency and cost
Reranking improves precision on good candidates — it cannot fix a broken first-stage retrieval

questions.prompt: Why can't a bi-encoder (standard vector search) match a cross-encoder's accuracy?
options: Bi-encoders use a smaller vocabulary
Bi-encoders embed the query and document separately, with no direct interaction between their tokens
Bi-encoders only work on short documents
Bi-encoders don't use transformers at all
questions.explanation: Bi-encoders compute embeddings independently and compare them with cosine similarity, so they can never model fine-grained interaction between specific query and document tokens the way a cross-encoder can.

questions.prompt: Why do production RAG systems rerank only the top 50-100 candidates instead of the whole corpus?
options: Cross-encoders can only process 50-100 documents ever, total
Reranking accuracy drops after 100 documents
Cross-encoder inference cost scales per candidate, so reranking everything would be too slow and expensive
Vector databases only return 50-100 results by design
questions.explanation: A cross-encoder runs a full forward pass per query-document pair, so cost and latency scale linearly with candidate count — reranking is only practical on a small shortlist from a cheap first-pass retrieval.

questions.prompt: If the right document never appears in vector search's top 100 results, what will reranking do?
options: It will still find and promote the correct document from the full corpus
It can only rerank what it's given — it cannot recover a document the first stage missed
It will automatically re-run vector search with a wider net
It will fall back to keyword search
questions.explanation: A reranker only scores the candidates it receives; it can't rescue relevance that the first-stage retrieval failed to surface at all, which is why first-stage recall still matters.

posts.title: AI-Trader: When Smarter Models Make Worse Traders
posts.subtitle: A live, uncontaminated benchmark shows six LLMs trade real markets — and intelligence doesn't guarantee profit.
posts.summary: AI-Trader is a live, data-uncontaminated benchmark that pits six mainstream LLMs against real-time U.S. stock, China A-share, and crypto markets with identical starting capital. Its central finding: general intelligence barely predicts trading performance — risk-control discipline does.

slides.title: Six LLMs. Real Markets. Real-Time Data.
bullets: Benchmark: AI-Trader (arXiv 2512.10971)
Live, data-uncontaminated evaluation — no backtesting on stale data
Tests U.S. stocks, China A-shares, and crypto with real-time feeds

slides.title: Every Prior Trading Benchmark Was Rigged by Hindsight
slides.body: Most LLM-trading papers backtest against historical data the model may have seen during pretraining, or replay markets where the outcome is already baked into the prompt. That flatters results in ways live deployment never would.
bullets: Backtests reuse price data the model has plausibly memorized in training
Static benchmarks can't capture regime shifts, trading halts, or slippage
No prior benchmark ran an agent against markets updating live, in real time

slides.title: A Live Feed, Three Markets, One Fixed Rulebook
slides.body: AI-Trader gives every agent the same $10,000 starting capital and the same live data feed, timed to real market clocks, so no agent can peek at the future.
bullets: Markets: NASDAQ-100 (U.S. stocks), SSE 50 (A-shares), 10 major crypto assets
Two trading granularities tested: daily and hourly decisions
Identical starting capital ($10,000) and tool access for every agent
Six mainstream LLMs evaluated head-to-head under identical conditions

slides.title: Some Markets Reward Agents More Than Others

rows: Highest liquidity — agents found excess returns most readily
Policy-driven moves made consistent alpha much harder to capture
Highest volatility — separated agents with real risk controls from those without

slides.title: The Same Model, Two Very Different Traders
slides.body: Picture Agent A: strong at reasoning benchmarks, but every dip triggers a full liquidation because its harness has no explicit stop-loss rule. Agent B: same base model, but its harness caps position size and never risks more than a small slice of capital per trade. AI-Trader's core finding is that harness-level risk discipline — not raw model IQ — decides which one survives a bad week.
bullets: Raw reasoning/benchmark strength did not reliably predict final portfolio value
Agents without explicit position-sizing rules were exposed to outsized drawdowns
Risk-control logic in the agent's harness mattered as much as model choice

slides.title: Where the Benchmark Still Falls Short
bullets: Six models is a small sample — one lucky or unlucky stretch can swing rankings
Simulated capital doesn't fully capture slippage from moving real, large size
Live evaluation is expensive to rerun, so leaderboard results can go stale
A benchmark score is not a license to point an agent at real trading capital

slides.title: The Real Lesson Isn't About Trading
bullets: Capability benchmarks and deployment-readiness are different questions
Explicit risk rules in the harness can matter more than the base model
'Live and data-uncontaminated' evaluation is becoming the new bar for agent benchmarks
Read the paper before trusting any 'LLM beats the market' headline

questions.prompt: What makes AI-Trader different from earlier LLM trading benchmarks?
options: It only tests one cryptocurrency
It evaluates agents live against real-time market data instead of static backtests
It only measures reasoning benchmarks, not trades
It requires no starting capital
questions.explanation: Live, data-uncontaminated evaluation is the benchmark's core contribution — it prevents agents from being graded on markets they could have memorized during training.

questions.prompt: According to the paper's key finding, what best predicts consistent trading performance?
options: The size of the base model
General reasoning benchmark scores
Risk-control capacity, not raw intelligence
The number of tools available to the agent
questions.explanation: The paper found risk control — not general intelligence — separated agents that held up across different market regimes from those that didn't.

questions.prompt: In which market did AI-Trader find agents captured excess returns most readily?
options: China A-shares (SSE 50)
U.S. stocks (NASDAQ-100)
Cryptocurrency
All markets equally
questions.explanation: The highly liquid U.S. market let agents find excess returns more easily than the policy-driven A-shares market.

references.title: AI-Trader: Benchmarking Autonomous Agents in Real-Time Financial Markets

posts.title: Gemini 3.5 Flash: Google's New Agentic Speed Model
posts.subtitle: Google's first Gemini 3.5 model tops Terminal-Bench and MCP Atlas while running about four times faster.
posts.summary: Google DeepMind's Gemini 3.5 Flash, announced at Google I/O 2026, outperforms last generation's flagship Gemini 3.1 Pro on coding and tool-calling benchmarks while running roughly 4x faster — making a Flash-tier model viable for serious agentic work for the first time.

slides.title: Google's Fastest Agentic Model Yet
bullets: Announced May 19, 2026 by Google DeepMind at Google I/O
First model in the new Gemini 3.5 family
Built for agentic coding, tool use, and long-horizon tasks

slides.title: Flash Models Used to Mean Trading Smarts for Speed
slides.body: Picking a 'Flash' tier model meant accepting a real capability gap versus the flagship Pro line — fine for simple chat, risky for multi-step agents that call tools in sequence.
bullets: Agentic workflows need many correct tool calls in a row — one bad call stalls the chain
Earlier Flash-tier models lagged flagship Pro models on coding and tool-use benchmarks
Developers had to choose between Pro's accuracy and Flash's speed and cost

slides.title: 3.5 Flash Beats Its Own Predecessor's Pro Model
slides.body: Gemini 3.5 Flash outperforms Gemini 3.1 Pro — last generation's flagship — on the exact benchmarks that matter for coding agents, while running about 4x faster.
bullets: Terminal-Bench 2.1 (real terminal coding tasks): 76.2%, vs. 71.4% for Gemini 3.1 Pro
MCP Atlas (multi-step tool-calling reliability): 83.6%
CharXiv Reasoning (multimodal chart understanding): 84.2%
Runs roughly 4x faster than other frontier-class models

slides.title: How It Stacks Up on Tool-Calling Reliability
What This Looks Like in an Agent Loop
slides.body: A coding agent wired to Gemini 3.5 Flash through MCP servers can chain file edits, test runs, and package installs with fewer stalls, because each individual tool call is more likely to be well-formed and issued in the right order.
bullets: Concrete task: 'fix the failing test, then open a PR' — a multi-step MCP tool chain
A higher MCP Atlas score means fewer malformed or out-of-order calls that break the chain
Running about 4x faster means more of these multi-step loops fit the same latency budget

slides.title: What Google Didn't Ship Yet
bullets: Gemini 3.5 Pro — the larger sibling — remained unreleased at announcement time
Benchmarks are self-reported by Google; independent replication takes time
'~4x faster' is relative to other frontier models, not an absolute latency spec
A high tool-calling score doesn't guarantee correctness of the underlying business logic

slides.title: Why This Release Matters
bullets: Flash-tier models are now viable for serious agentic coding, not just simple chat
MCP Atlas is emerging as a benchmark to watch for tool-calling reliability
Available now in Google AI Studio, Android Studio, Gemini Enterprise, and the Gemini app
Expect Gemini 3.5 Pro to push these numbers further once it ships

questions.prompt: What is Gemini 3.5 Flash's key claimed advantage over its predecessor line?
options: It's cheaper but less capable at everything
It beats the prior generation's flagship Pro model on coding/agentic benchmarks while running faster
It only works for image generation
It replaces the Gemini app entirely
questions.explanation: Google reported Gemini 3.5 Flash outperforming Gemini 3.1 Pro on Terminal-Bench 2.1 while running about 4x faster — a Flash-tier model beating a prior flagship.

questions.prompt: What does the MCP Atlas benchmark measure?
options: Image generation quality
How reliably a model calls tools correctly across sequences of dependent calls
Translation accuracy
Model size in parameters
questions.explanation: MCP Atlas evaluates multi-step, dependent tool-calling reliability — directly relevant to agents built on Model Context Protocol servers.

questions.prompt: On Terminal-Bench 2.1, how did Gemini 3.5 Flash compare to Gemini 3.1 Pro?
options: Scored lower (65% vs. 71.4%)
Scored about the same
Scored higher (76.2% vs. 71.4%)
Wasn't tested on this benchmark
questions.explanation: Gemini 3.5 Flash scored 76.2% versus Gemini 3.1 Pro's 71.4% — a Flash-tier model outscoring last generation's Pro-tier flagship.

posts.title: Test-Time Compute: Buying Accuracy With More Thinking
posts.subtitle: Why letting a model 'think longer' at inference can beat training a bigger one.
posts.summary: Test-time compute scaling improves LLM accuracy by spending more compute at inference — longer reasoning chains, multiple samples, or search over reasoning paths — instead of training a bigger model. It's the mechanism behind modern 'reasoning mode' toggles, and it comes with real latency and cost tradeoffs.

slides.title: More Thinking, Not More Parameters
bullets: A second axis for scaling LLMs: compute spent at inference, not training
Powers reasoning-style models and best-of-N sampling techniques
Can turn a smaller model into a better one — for a price per query

slides.title: Training-Time Scaling Was Hitting Diminishing Returns
slides.body: For years, 'bigger model, more data' was the dominant lever. But each further jump in pretraining compute buys smaller accuracy gains, while GPU and data supply get more expensive and scarcer.
bullets: Scaling laws show shrinking marginal gains per added parameter at fixed data
High-quality pretraining data is finite and increasingly exhausted
A single fixed-size model gives the same effort whether a question is trivial or brutally hard

slides.title: Spend Compute at Answer Time Instead
slides.body: Instead of only making the model bigger, let it use more compute per query: generate longer chains of reasoning, sample multiple candidate answers, or search over intermediate steps before committing to a final one.
bullets: Chain-of-thought: generate intermediate reasoning tokens before the final answer
Best-of-N / self-consistency: sample N answers, keep the majority or highest-scored one
Search-based methods (tree-of-thought style): explore and backtrack over reasoning branches
A verifier or reward model can rank candidate reasoning paths before one is chosen

slides.title: A Concrete Trade: One Pass vs. a Reasoning Budget
slides.body: Ask a model a hard competition-math problem. A single greedy-decode pass might get it wrong. Give the same frozen model an inference budget to generate many independent reasoning chains and vote on the most common final answer, and accuracy on hard math and coding benchmarks tends to rise — without touching a single training weight.
bullets: Same frozen weights, same training run — only inference-time compute changes
Cost scales with the number of samples or reasoning steps allowed
This is the mechanism behind 'reasoning mode' toggles in modern chat products

slides.title: Two Scaling Levers, Compared

rows: More GPUs, more data, one-time training cost
Higher ceiling on every query, even easy ones
Higher latency and cost per query, no retraining
Better accuracy on hard queries; easy queries barely benefit
Current state-of-the-art reasoning models use both levers together

slides.title: When Test-Time Compute Is the Wrong Lever
bullets: Latency-sensitive products (autocomplete, real-time chat) can't afford long hidden reasoning
Easy queries gain almost nothing — you pay a compute tax on questions already answered correctly
Cost multiplies per user query, unlike training cost which is paid once
A miscalibrated verifier or reward model can make best-of-N confidently pick a wrong answer

slides.title: The Two Scaling Axes, Side by Side
bullets: Train-time compute buys a smarter model for every future query
Test-time compute rents extra accuracy for one query at a time
Route hard queries to high-compute reasoning, easy queries to fast single-pass decoding
That routing decision is itself becoming a core system-design problem for LLM products

questions.prompt: What is 'test-time compute' scaling in the context of LLMs?
options: Training a larger model on more data
Spending more compute at inference time (e.g., longer reasoning chains, multiple samples) to improve an answer
Reducing the model's parameter count to save memory
A method for compressing the KV cache
questions.explanation: Test-time compute scaling improves output quality by using more compute per query at inference — not by changing the trained model itself.

questions.prompt: Why does best-of-N sampling with self-consistency often improve accuracy on hard reasoning tasks?
options: It always picks the first generated answer
Majority voting across independent reasoning attempts cancels out some random errors
It retrains the model on the sampled answers
It reduces the number of tokens generated
questions.explanation: Sampling multiple independent reasoning chains and taking the majority (or highest-scored) answer reduces the impact of any single chain going wrong.

questions.prompt: When is test-time compute scaling the WRONG lever to reach for?
options: When a query is extremely difficult, like competition math
When latency budgets are tight and the query is already easy for the model
When you have a verifier model available
When you want better accuracy on hard benchmarks
questions.explanation: Easy queries gain little from extra reasoning compute, and latency-sensitive products can't absorb the added cost and delay — so blanket test-time scaling wastes resources there.

references.title: Scaling LLM Test-Time Compute Optimally can be More Effective than Scaling Model Parameters

posts.title: AlayaWorld: A World Model You Can Play for a Minute
posts.subtitle: Open-source, chunk-by-chunk video generation holds a coherent scene past 60 seconds of interactive play.
posts.summary: AlayaWorld is an open-source interactive world model, fine-tuned from LTX-2.3, that generates a playable, camera-controllable video environment and keeps it spatially coherent for over a minute -- far longer than prior public world models, which visibly drift within seconds. It gets there with an explicit 3D cache for spatial memory, an error bank that deliberately re-injects artifacts during training, and few-step distillation for near real-time interaction.

slides.title: A World You Can Walk Through -- Open Source, for Real
slides.body: Posted July 7, 2026 and #1 on Hugging Face Daily Papers by the next morning, AlayaWorld is a full-stack open release: architecture, code, and a live demo of an interactive video world.

slides.title: Interactive World Models Usually Fall Apart Fast
slides.body: Autoregressive video world models generate the next frame conditioned on the last -- and every generated frame is slightly wrong. Feed that error back in as input for the next frame and it compounds.
bullets: Most public interactive world models stay coherent for only a handful of seconds before textures smear or geometry drifts
Revisiting a location seen earlier is the hardest case -- the model has to remember what it generated, not just imitate real video
No open, full-stack (architecture + code) baseline existed for research on long-horizon playable generation

slides.title: Chunked Generation With a Memory the Model Can Query
slides.body: AlayaWorld generates video in roughly one-second chunks, each produced with just 4 denoising steps for near real-time interaction, fine-tuned from LTX-2.3.
bullets: 3D cache: an explicit spatial memory, modulated with AdaLN-style camera control, so re-entering a room pulls back what was already generated there
Compressed frame-history embeddings carry short-term temporal continuity between chunks
Chunk-level prompt switching lets a new event or instruction enter mid-generation without restarting the scene

slides.title: The Error Bank: Training the Model to Absorb Its Own Mistakes
slides.body: Instead of training only on clean ground-truth video, AlayaWorld deliberately re-injects previously generated drift artifacts back into the training targets.
bullets: A bank of accumulated generation errors is sampled and fed back in as if it were real history
This forces the model to learn to recover from imperfect self-generated context, not just extend perfect video
The team frames it as choosing to absorb error rather than trying to architect it away entirely

slides.title: Walking a Loop Through a Generated Room
slides.body: Concretely: a user steers the camera out of a room, down a hallway, and back -- a round trip of roughly 30-40 seconds.
bullets: Without a spatial cache, the room the user returns to would be regenerated from scratch and look different
With the 3D cache, the furniture, lighting, and layout the model already committed to are pulled back and rendered consistently
This is exactly the failure mode the paper targets: coherence isn't hard for 5 seconds, it's hard once you leave and come back

slides.title: What's Actually Shipped vs. What's Promised
slides.body: AlayaWorld is Apache-2.0 and the architecture is fully described, but treat it as a research release, not a drop-in product.
bullets: Weights and training code are not yet public -- the GitHub repo lists inference code as 'coming soon'
Real-time claims depend on the 4-step distilled decoder; quality still trails slower, many-step diffusion sampling
'Long-horizon' means past a minute, not indefinite -- the paper doesn't claim unbounded coherence
Best fit today: robotics/embodied-AI simulation and world-model research, not consumer video generation

slides.title: Why This Paper Matters
bullets: First open, full-stack world model to sustain playable interaction past the one-minute mark
The 3D cache + error bank combination is a reusable recipe for any autoregressive generator that suffers from drift
Signals where world models are heading: usable simulators for training embodied agents, not just tech demos

questions.prompt: Why does error compound in autoregressive world models?
options: Each frame is generated conditioned on the model's own previous (slightly imperfect) output
The model uses too high a resolution
The camera control module is undertrained
GPUs round floating point numbers differently each step
questions.explanation: Autoregressive generation feeds each output back in as input for the next step, so small per-frame errors accumulate over time unless something explicitly corrects for it.

questions.prompt: What does AlayaWorld's 3D cache mainly solve?
options: Reducing training data size
Keeping previously generated locations visually consistent when revisited
Compressing the model for mobile deployment
Speeding up text prompt parsing
questions.explanation: The 3D cache is spatial memory -- when the camera returns to an earlier location, the cache lets the model reproduce what it already generated there instead of hallucinating something new.

questions.prompt: What is the 'error bank' technique?
options: A hardware cache for GPU memory errors
Deliberately re-injecting the model's own drifted generation history into training so it learns to recover from imperfect context
A dataset of failed training runs used for debugging
A safety filter that rejects low-quality frames at inference time
questions.explanation: Rather than training only on clean video, AlayaWorld's error bank feeds accumulated generation artifacts back into training -- teaching the model to stay coherent even when its own recent context is imperfect.

references.title: AlayaWorld: Interactive Long-Horizon World Modeling Toward Generative Reality (arXiv:2607.06291)
AlayaWorld GitHub Repository

posts.title: MCP Goes Stateless: 2026's Biggest Protocol Rewrite
posts.subtitle: Sessions are gone, Tasks and Apps ship as extensions, and Sampling is on a 12-month exit.
posts.summary: The MCP 2026-07-28 specification -- locked as a release candidate and shipping final on July 28 -- is the largest revision since the protocol launched. It removes the session handshake in favor of a stateless core that runs behind ordinary load balancers, splits Tasks and UI rendering into versioned extensions, tightens OAuth/OIDC alignment, and starts a 12-month clock on deprecating Roots, Sampling, and MCP-specific logging.

slides.title: MCP's Session Model Just Got Removed
slides.body: The Model Context Protocol's 2026-07-28 spec -- release candidate locked May 21, final on July 28 -- is described by its own maintainers as the largest revision since launch.

slides.title: Sessions Didn't Scale Past a Single Server
slides.body: The original MCP handshake -- initialize/initialized plus an Mcp-Session-Id header -- pinned a client to one server instance for the life of a connection.
bullets: Remote deployments needed sticky sessions, a shared session store, and deep packet inspection at the gateway just to route requests correctly
That's infrastructure a stateless HTTP API never needs -- and it made MCP servers harder to run at scale than the APIs they were wrapping
Server-initiated requests (like asking the model for more input) relied on long-lived Server-Sent Events connections

slides.title: Every Request Now Carries Its Own Context
slides.body: The initialize handshake and Mcp-Session-Id are removed entirely. Protocol version, client info, and capabilities now travel in _meta fields on each individual request.
bullets: Any MCP request can land on any server instance -- a remote server can now sit behind a plain round-robin load balancer
New Mcp-Method and Mcp-Name headers let load balancers and rate-limiters route on the operation without inspecting the request body
MCP-Protocol-Version replaces session-based version negotiation

slides.title: Tasks and UI Rendering Become Extensions, Not Core
slides.body: Two official extensions ship alongside the stateless core, each with its own reverse-DNS identifier and independent versioning.
bullets: MCP Apps: servers deliver interactive HTML interfaces that hosts render in a sandboxed iframe, with actions routed through the same audit path as direct tool calls
Tasks: long-running tools/call requests return a task handle; clients drive progress with tasks/get, tasks/update, and tasks/cancel instead of holding a connection open
Server-to-client prompts switch to Multi-Round-Trip Requests -- an InputRequiredResult the client answers by re-issuing the call with inputResponses, which any server instance can pick up

slides.title: Three Features Get a 12-Month Exit Ramp
slides.body: All three carry a formal twelve-month removal window under the spec's new deprecation policy -- the first time MCP has committed to one.

rows: Tool parameters, resource URIs, or server configuration
Direct integration with an LLM provider API
stderr for stdio servers; OpenTelemetry for structured observability

slides.title: Caching a Tool List Without a Persistent Connection
slides.body: Concretely: a gateway serving thousands of clients no longer needs to track which server instance holds which session.
bullets: List and resource responses now carry ttlMs and cacheScope metadata, explicitly modeled on HTTP Cache-Control
A client can cache a tools/list response for as long as ttlMs allows and skip re-fetching it on every call
Tool schemas also gain full JSON Schema 2020-12 support -- oneOf/anyOf/allOf compositions and $ref/$defs -- so a tool's input contract can express real conditional logic

slides.title: This Is a Breaking Change, Not a Patch
slides.body: Existing servers built around the session handshake, Sampling, or Roots have real migration work ahead.
bullets: Servers using Sampling to route completions through the client's LLM now need direct provider API access -- a real architectural change, not a config flag
The ten-week window from RC to final is meant for SDK maintainers to validate against real workloads; only Tier 1 SDKs (Python, TypeScript, Go, C#) are guaranteed support in that window
Error codes also change (missing resources now return standard JSON-RPC -32602 instead of MCP's own -32002), which will break brittle error-message matching
If you're maintaining a production MCP server today, plan the migration -- don't wait for July 28 to read the changelog

slides.title: The Takeaway
bullets: MCP is trading session affinity for horizontal scalability -- the protocol now behaves like a normal stateless HTTP API
Extensions (Apps, Tasks) let the spec grow without re-litigating the core on every release
Sampling's deprecation is notable: routing LLM calls through the client is being replaced by servers calling providers directly
W3C Trace Context propagation (traceparent/tracestate/baggage) is now formally part of the spec -- a single trace can follow a tool call end-to-end

questions.prompt: What replaces the Mcp-Session-Id handshake in the 2026-07-28 spec?
options: Nothing -- sessions are still required
Per-request _meta fields plus Mcp-Method/Mcp-Name headers, so any request can land on any server instance
A single global API key
A WebSocket connection that stays open for the client's lifetime
questions.explanation: The stateless core moves protocol metadata onto individual requests instead of a connection-scoped handshake, which is what allows requests to be load-balanced across any server instance.

questions.prompt: What is ttlMs on a tools/list response modeled on?
options: Database transaction timeouts
HTTP Cache-Control
TCP keepalive intervals
OAuth token expiry
questions.explanation: ttlMs and cacheScope metadata are explicitly modeled on HTTP's Cache-Control semantics, letting clients cache list/resource responses instead of re-fetching them.

questions.prompt: Which feature is being deprecated in favor of servers calling an LLM provider's API directly?
options: Tasks
MCP Apps
Sampling
Tool schemas
questions.explanation: Sampling -- where a server asked the connected client to run a completion -- is deprecated with a 12-month window; the replacement is direct LLM provider integration on the server side.
