posts.title: Function Calling: Teaching an LLM to Use Real Tools
posts.subtitle: A JSON schema, a model trained to fill it in, and a loop that feeds the result back.
posts.summary: Function calling lets a model act on the world by emitting a structured request instead of prose -- the harness executes it and feeds the result back in. It's the mechanism underneath nearly every AI agent, from a weather bot to MCP-connected tool use, and understanding its failure modes explains why agents with 50 tools often behave worse than agents with 5.

slides.title: The Model Never Actually Calls Anything
slides.body: Function calling is a naming trap: the LLM doesn't execute code. It emits structured data that your code decides whether to execute.

slides.title: Three Pieces: a Schema, a Trained Model, a Loop
slides.body: You describe available tools as JSON Schema (name, description, parameter types). The model is fine-tuned to recognize when a tool fits the user's request and to emit a matching JSON object instead of a plain-text answer.
bullets: The schema is just documentation the model reads -- there's no guarantee it fills in valid, safe, or even sensible arguments
Your application code -- not the model -- parses that JSON and actually runs the function, API call, or query
The result goes back into the conversation as a 'tool' message; the model reads it and writes the final answer

slides.title: Why a Trained Format Beats Prompted JSON
slides.body: Early tool-use patterns (like ReAct) just asked the model to output JSON inside its regular text -- fragile, easy to malform.
bullets: Modern APIs (Claude, GPT, Gemini) constrain decoding so the model can only emit tokens that satisfy the tool's JSON schema -- a schema violation becomes structurally impossible, not just less likely
A tool-choice setting can force a specific tool, force any tool, or leave it optional -- useful when you know the next step must be a lookup
Models can request several tool calls in one turn (parallel tool calls) instead of one call per round trip, cutting latency for independent lookups

slides.title: A Weather Agent, Turn by Turn
slides.body: User asks: 'Should I bring an umbrella to Austin tomorrow?'
bullets: Turn 1 -- model emits a call: get_forecast with arguments city=Austin, date=tomorrow, instead of guessing the weather
Your code calls the real weather API, gets back precip_chance=70, condition=thunderstorms, and appends it as a tool-result message
Turn 2 -- model reads that result and writes: 'Yes -- 70% chance of thunderstorms in Austin tomorrow, bring one.' It never saw real weather data until you gave it back

slides.title: Structured Tool Calls vs. Prompted JSON

rows: None -- model can emit malformed JSON or wrap it in prose
Native -- multiple calls in one turn

slides.title: Where Function Calling Breaks Down
slides.body: A valid JSON schema doesn't mean a correct decision.
bullets: Tool selection degrades with scale: past roughly 20-30 tools in context, models start picking the wrong one or ignoring relevant ones entirely
Every tool definition costs context tokens on every turn -- a large tool library can eat thousands of tokens before the conversation even starts
Arguments can be confidently wrong -- a syntactically valid call with a hallucinated parameter (wrong city, invented ID) still executes if you don't validate it
Not every tool call should auto-execute -- a delete_record or send_email tool needs a confirmation step, not blind trust in the schema match

slides.title: The Takeaway
bullets: Function calling is a formatting contract, not an execution guarantee -- your code still decides what actually runs
Constrained decoding turned 'ask the model for JSON' into 'the model structurally cannot emit invalid JSON' -- that reliability jump is what made real agents practical
Curate your tool list; more tools in context usually means worse tool selection, not more capability
This is the exact mechanism MCP servers plug into -- MCP standardizes how tools are described and discovered, function calling is how the model actually invokes them

questions.prompt: What does the LLM actually do in function calling?
options: Directly executes code in a sandbox
Emits a structured JSON object describing a tool call, which application code then executes
Compiles and runs the function itself
Sends an HTTP request directly to the API
questions.explanation: The model only produces structured data describing what it wants to call and with what arguments -- your application code is what actually executes it.

questions.prompt: What does constrained decoding guarantee in native function calling?
options: The arguments will always be factually correct
The output will always be a JSON object that validates against the declared schema
The tool will always succeed when executed
The model will always pick the most efficient tool
questions.explanation: Constrained decoding restricts which tokens the model can produce so the output structurally matches the schema -- it guarantees valid shape, not correct or safe content.

questions.prompt: Why can adding more tools to an agent hurt performance?
options: More tools always improve accuracy with no downside
Each tool definition costs context tokens and, past a few dozen tools, models start selecting the wrong one or ignoring relevant tools
Tools can only be added in pairs
Adding tools disables parallel tool calls
questions.explanation: Every tool schema in context costs tokens on every turn, and tool-selection accuracy measurably degrades once the tool list grows past roughly 20-30 options.

posts.title: MCP Sampling & Elicitation: When the Server Gets to Ask
posts.subtitle: Most of MCP is the server answering — these two primitives let it ask instead
posts.summary: Sampling (sampling/createMessage) lets an MCP server borrow the client's LLM mid-task, and elicitation (elicitation/create) lets it pause to ask the user a structured question — both are nested JSON-RPC requests that flip MCP's usual client-to-server direction of control.

slides.title: MCP Sampling & Elicitation: When the Server Gets to Ask
slides.body: Most of MCP is the server answering the client's requests. Sampling and elicitation flip that: the server can ask the client for a model completion, or ask the user for missing input, in the middle of handling a call.

slides.title: Tools and Resources Only Flow One Way
slides.body: A standard MCP tool call is client to server to result. That breaks down the moment a server needs to reason mid-task or needs information the original call never included.
bullets: A summarization server wants to condense scraped text before returning it — but it has no LLM of its own
A bug-filing tool gets a description with no severity, and guessing wrong creates a bad ticket
Forcing every server to hold its own API key, or every client to guess all inputs upfront, doesn't scale

slides.title: Sampling: sampling/createMessage Borrows the Client's Model
slides.body: Sampling is a server-to-client JSON-RPC request sent while the server is still handling the client's original call. It hands over a messages array, an optional system prompt, and model preferences — the client's host actually runs the inference and returns the completion.
bullets: Request carries messages[], an optional systemPrompt, and maxTokens — the server never needs its own model or API key
The client's host decides which model actually answers; the server only expresses preferences
It's nested inside the in-flight tool call, so the server can act on the completion before returning its final result

slides.title: Elicitation: elicitation/create Pauses to Ask the User
slides.body: Elicitation lets a server request specific structured input mid-operation. It sends a human-readable message plus a requestedSchema, the client renders a small form, and the user can accept, decline, or cancel.
bullets: Three response states: accept (data provided), decline (explicit no), cancel (dismissed without choosing)
requestedSchema is intentionally limited to flat primitives — string, number, boolean, enum — no nested objects or arrays
That limit keeps every MCP client's form-renderer simple: no client has to build a general JSON Schema UI engine

slides.title: The Flow Runs Server, Then Client, Then Human, Then Back
slides.body: Both features are nested requests: they happen inside the lifetime of the tool call the client already made, not as a separate exchange.
bullets: 1. Client calls tools/call on the server
2. Mid-execution, the server sends sampling/createMessage or elicitation/create back to the client
3. The client's host surfaces it — runs the model, or shows the user a form
4. The result flows back as the response to that nested request, and the server resumes and finishes the original tool call

slides.title: Worked Example: A Bug-Filing Server That's Missing a Priority
slides.body: An MCP server exposes a file_bug tool. The client calls it with just a description — no severity field. Instead of guessing or erroring out, the server elicits the missing piece.
bullets: Server sends elicitation/create: message 'What priority should this bug have?', schema = enum [low, medium, high, critical]
Client renders a small form; the user picks 'high'
Server receives { action: 'accept', content: { priority: 'high' } } and finishes filing the ticket with a complete payload
If the user hits cancel instead, the server can fall back to a sane default rather than failing the whole call

slides.title: Four MCP Primitives, Four Directions of Control

rows: Server performs an action or computation
Client's LLM completes a request for the server
User fills a small form for the server

slides.title: The Catch: Both Require a Client That Opted In
slides.body: Sampling and elicitation only work if the client declares support for them during initialization — plenty of MCP clients still support neither, and a server can't force the issue.
bullets: A server must gracefully degrade if the client never advertised sampling/elicitation capabilities
Elicitation must never be used to request passwords, tokens, or other sensitive data — the spec calls this out explicitly
A trustworthy host always shows WHICH server is asking before a sampling or elicitation prompt reaches the user
Because both are nested inside a live tool call, a slow or unresponsive user turns into a hung call — servers should set sane timeouts

questions.prompt: What does an MCP server actually receive back after sending an elicitation/create request that the user approves?
options: Just an "ok" boolean
An object with action: "accept" and a content field matching the requested schema
The raw HTML of the rendered form
Nothing — elicitation is fire-and-forget
questions.explanation: Accept responses always include a content field shaped by the schema the server requested — that's what lets the server resume with real structured data instead of a guess.

questions.prompt: Why can't an elicitation requestedSchema include a nested object or an array of objects?
options: JSON-RPC doesn't support nested objects
It's a deliberate limit to keep every client's form-rendering UI simple
MCP servers aren't allowed to define custom schemas
Nested schemas would leak into the tool's return value
questions.explanation: The spec intentionally restricts elicitation schemas to flat primitives and enums so any client can render a basic form without implementing a full JSON Schema UI engine.

questions.prompt: What's the key structural difference between a sampling/createMessage request and a normal tools/call?
options: Sampling is initiated by the SERVER, nested inside a tool call already in progress
Sampling always costs more tokens than a tool call
Sampling can only be used by resources, never by tools
Sampling responses are cached but tool call responses are not
questions.explanation: Direction is the whole point: tools/call flows client-to-server, but sampling/createMessage flows server-to-client, asking the host's own model to do work on the server's behalf.

posts.title: LingBot-Video: A 30B MoE Brain Built to Move Robots
posts.subtitle: Why a video model trained to look pretty makes a bad robot simulator — and how sparse experts fix it
posts.summary: LingBot-Video is a DiT-based video pretraining model built specifically for embodied intelligence: a 30B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts network that only activates 3B parameters per token, trained on 70,000+ hours of robot-augmented footage, and it tops the open-source RBench leaderboard for physically plausible video generation.

slides.title: LingBot-Video: One Model, Two Sizes, Robot-Ready
slides.body: A new open-source video pretraining paradigm asks: what if the video generator wasn't optimized to look beautiful, but to be physically correct enough for a robot to plan on?

slides.title: Cinematic Video Models Don't Understand Physics
slides.body: Today's best video diffusion models are trained on internet video to maximize visual fidelity and creativity — not physical realism. That's a domain mismatch when you want to use the model as a 'world model' for robot planning.
bullets: Internet video is optimized for aesthetics: lighting, composition, motion blur
Robots need the OPPOSITE priority: consistent physics, accurate contact, predictable object permanence
A gorgeous but physically wrong prediction is worse than a plain but correct one for planning

slides.title: 30B Total Parameters, Only 3B Active Per Token
slides.body: LingBot-Video ships two variants: a 1.3B dense model and a 30B-A3B Mixture-of-Experts model built on a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) backbone. The MoE router activates just 3B parameters per token, so you get near-30B capacity at a fraction of the compute.
bullets: Naming convention '30B-A3B' = 30B total params, ~3B Active per forward pass
MoE trades a memory cost (all 30B weights must be resident) for a compute win
Result: roughly 3x faster inference than a dense model of comparable capability

slides.title: 70,000+ Hours of Robot Footage, Blended Into Web Video
slides.body: A data profiling engine deliberately augments the standard internet-video pretraining mix with manipulation, navigation, and egocentric robot footage, so the base model picks up an intrinsic sense of actions and world dynamics before any task-specific fine-tuning.
bullets: Manipulation: arm/gripper contact, grasping, object deformation
Navigation: egocentric locomotion, obstacle avoidance
A multi-dimensional reward system further tunes for aesthetics AND physical rationality AND task completion — not just one of those

slides.title: It Tops the Open-Source Leaderboard on RBench
slides.body: On the RBench leaderboard for embodied video generation, LingBot-Video's MoE variant posts the best average score among open-source models, with its biggest edge on quadruped locomotion.

slides.title: Worked Example: Simulating Before You Actuate
slides.body: A robotics team wants to test whether a gripper can pick up a mug without knocking over a nearby glass. Instead of risking real hardware, they feed the current camera frame plus a candidate action into LingBot-Video.
bullets: Model rolls out ~1-2 seconds of predicted video showing the likely outcome
If the glass tips in the prediction, the planner discards that action and tries another
This is model-based planning: use the generative model as a cheap, fast simulator instead of real trials

slides.title: The Catch: It's a Predictor, Not a Physics Engine
slides.body: Sparse MoE models don't save you on memory — you still need enough VRAM to hold all 30B weights, even though only 3B compute per token. And a generated video, however physically plausible, is still a prediction, not ground truth.
bullets: VRAM footprint scales with TOTAL params (30B), not active params (3B) — plan hardware accordingly
Confidently wrong predictions can mislead a planner just as badly as confidently wrong language
Best used as one signal in a planning loop, not a standalone source of truth
Released under Apache 2.0 with both dense (1.3B) and MoE (30B-A3B) checkpoints on Hugging Face

questions.prompt: In LingBot-Video's '30B-A3B' MoE variant, how many parameters actually run for a given token?
options: 3B
questions.explanation: The 'A3B' suffix means ~3B parameters are Active per token — the MoE router sends each token to only a subset of the 30B total experts, which is where the inference speedup comes from.

questions.prompt: Why do generic internet-trained video diffusion models make poor 'world models' for robot planning?
options: They are too slow to run in real time
They are optimized for visual fidelity and creativity, not physical accuracy
They cannot generate video longer than 1 second
They require labeled bounding boxes to run
questions.explanation: Internet video pretraining rewards aesthetics — good lighting, composition, style — not physical consistency. A robot planner needs the opposite priority: predictable, physically correct outcomes, even if less visually polished.

questions.prompt: What's a key hardware tradeoff of the MoE design compared to a dense model of similar quality?
options: MoE needs less VRAM because only 3B params run at once
MoE needs enough VRAM to hold all 30B weights even though only ~3B compute per token, trading memory for ~3x faster inference
MoE and dense models have identical memory and speed
MoE can only run on CPU, not GPU
questions.explanation: Sparse MoE saves compute, not memory: all expert weights must stay resident in VRAM for routing to work, even though each token only activates a fraction of them. The payoff here is roughly 3x faster inference than a similarly capable dense model.

posts.title: Nano Banana 2 Lite & Gemini Omni Flash Ship Together
posts.subtitle: Google's new pair: a 4-second image model feeding a conversational video editor, priced to match Veo 3.1 Fast
posts.summary: Google shipped two new generative media models together: Nano Banana 2 Lite, a sub-4-second, $0.034-per-image model for text-to-image, and Gemini Omni Flash, a conversational video generation/editing model priced at $0.10 per second of output — designed to chain into a single image-to-video creative pipeline.

slides.title: Two Models, One Creative Pipeline
slides.body: Google Cloud released Nano Banana 2 Lite (image) and Gemini Omni Flash (video) as a matched pair — generate a still fast and cheap, then animate it with a sentence.

slides.title: Iterating on Generative Media Was Too Slow, Too Pricy
slides.body: Developers building creative and marketing tools needed a fast, cheap image loop that could hand off directly into video — without switching models, formats, or APIs mid-pipeline.
bullets: Slow generation kills iterative creative workflows (mockups, ad variants, product shots)
Existing high-quality video models were priced for one-off hero shots, not rapid iteration
No easy image-to-video handoff meant stitching together separate tools

slides.title: Nano Banana 2 Lite: 4-Second Images at $0.034 Each
slides.body: Nano Banana 2 Lite (API name gemini-3.1-flash-lite-image) reached general availability on June 30, 2026, as Google's fastest, cheapest Gemini image model — positioned as the direct upgrade path from the original Nano Banana (gemini-2.5-flash-image).
bullets: Text-to-image generation in as little as 4 seconds
$0.034 per 1K-resolution image
Strong on world knowledge (contextual scenes, data visualizations, localized mockups) and character consistency across generations

slides.title: Gemini Omni Flash: Conversational Video Editing
slides.body: Gemini Omni Flash launched in public preview the same day: a video generation and editing model priced at $0.10 per second of output — matching Veo 3.1 Fast pricing — that accepts text, image, and video as input and generates up to 10 seconds per clip.
bullets: Conversational editing: character swaps, relighting, camera-angle changes via plain-language instructions
Native audio generated with every video output, not a separate step
Built-in physics simulation and world knowledge for legible text/graphics and synced actions

slides.title: The Two Models Side by Side
Worked Example: Product Shot to Ad in One Pipeline
slides.body: An e-commerce team generates a product photo with Nano Banana 2 Lite in ~4 seconds, then passes that same image into Gemini Omni Flash as a reference frame to animate it into a 10-second ad clip with synced narration audio — no separate video shoot.
bullets: Early integration partners include Adobe (Firefly), Figma, Invideo, WPP, Artlist, and Manus AI
Image-to-video handoff is the explicit design goal: one reference image seeds the animated result
Useful for rapid ad-variant testing where a dozen concepts need to ship same-day

slides.title: Watermarking Is On by Default — and Some Features Aren't Live Yet
slides.body: Both models embed C2PA content credentials and imperceptible SynthID watermarks by default, which matters for compliance workflows. Omni Flash is still preview: several announced features aren't shipped yet.
bullets: C2PA + SynthID watermarking cannot be turned off — plan for it in any pipeline that needs 'clean' output
Omni Flash 'coming soon': audio references, video references, last-frame conditioning, scene extension, higher resolutions
Provisioned throughput is available now for Nano Banana 2 Lite, still rolling out for Omni Flash
If your workflow needs those missing features today, you're not there yet — plan around the preview status

A mandatory content moderation delay

options: ~1 second
~4 seconds

posts.title: ReAct: Reasoning and Acting in the Same Loop
posts.subtitle: The pattern that turned language models from answerers into agents — one Thought, Action, Observation at a time
posts.summary: ReAct interleaves explicit reasoning steps ('Thought') with tool calls ('Action') and their results ('Observation') in a single loop, letting a language model plan, gather real information, and correct course — the pattern underneath almost every modern LLM agent.

slides.title: Thought. Action. Observation. Repeat.
slides.body: ReAct (2022) is the loop that most agent frameworks still run today: the model reasons about what to do, takes an action in the world, reads the result, and reasons again.

slides.title: Chain-of-Thought Reasons; It Doesn't Check Its Work
slides.body: Chain-of-thought prompting gets a model to reason step by step, but every fact it uses comes from its own frozen training data. There's no way to fetch new information or verify a claim mid-reasoning, so errors compound silently.
bullets: Pure CoT hallucinates facts and can't recover once it's committed to a wrong premise
Pure action-taking (no reasoning) executes tools blindly with no plan or self-correction
Neither alone handles multi-step tasks that need both a plan and real-world facts

slides.title: Interleave Reasoning Traces With Tool Calls
slides.body: ReAct prompts the model to alternate: a Thought (free-text reasoning about what to do next), an Action (a tool call, like a search query), and an Observation (the tool's real result) — then loops, feeding each observation back into the next Thought.
bullets: Thought: 'I need the year this company was founded'
Action: search('Company X founding year')
Observation: '...founded in 1998...' — now grounded in a real fact
Thought: 'Now I can answer' → final answer

slides.title: Why Grounding the Reasoning Trace Matters
slides.body: Because each Thought is conditioned on a real Observation rather than the model's own possibly-wrong prior step, ReAct catches and corrects errors mid-trajectory instead of confidently reasoning forward from a hallucination.
bullets: The reasoning trace becomes interpretable: you can see exactly which fact drove which conclusion
Mistakes surface as a bad Observation the model can react to, not a silent wrong assumption
This is the same loop underneath tool-using chatbots, coding agents, and research agents today

slides.title: The Original Numbers Still Hold Up
slides.body: In the original paper, few-shot ReAct prompting (as little as 1-2 examples) beat imitation- and reinforcement-learning baselines trained on thousands to hundreds of thousands of task instances.

rows: +34% absolute success rate vs. imitation/RL trained on 10^3-10^5 instances
+10% absolute success rate vs. same baselines
Reduces hallucination vs. pure CoT by grounding steps in Wikipedia API calls

slides.title: Worked Example: A Multi-Hop Question
slides.body: Question: 'What year was the founder of the company that makes the iPhone born?' A pure-CoT model might guess. A ReAct loop instead: Thought → search 'iPhone maker' → Observation 'Apple Inc.' → Thought → search 'Apple founder' → Observation 'Steve Jobs' → Thought → search 'Steve Jobs birth year' → Observation '1955' → final answer.
bullets: Each hop is a separate, verifiable Action instead of one unverified guess
If an early Observation is wrong or ambiguous, the next Thought can re-plan (e.g., search again, pick a different source)
This is exactly the shape of modern 'agentic search' features in chat products

slides.title: The Cost: Every Step Is Another Model Call
slides.body: ReAct's grounding comes at a real price — latency and token cost scale with the number of Thought/Action/Observation cycles, and a bad tool (unreliable search, buggy API) still poisons the trajectory.
bullets: Each loop iteration is a full model round-trip: 5 hops means 5x the latency of a single answer
Garbage Observations in → garbage Thoughts out; the pattern grounds against your tools, not against truth itself
Best for tasks that genuinely need external, changing, or verifiable information — skip it for simple lookups a single call can answer
Modern function-calling APIs are effectively a structured, production-grade implementation of this same loop

questions.prompt: What are the three repeating steps in the ReAct loop?
options: Plan, Execute, Report
Thought, Action, Observation
Query, Retrieve, Generate
Input, Process, Output
questions.explanation: ReAct interleaves a Thought (reasoning), an Action (a tool call), and an Observation (the tool's real result), then loops — each new Thought is conditioned on the latest Observation.

questions.prompt: Why does grounding reasoning in real Observations reduce hallucination compared to pure chain-of-thought?
options: It uses a larger model automatically
Each reasoning step can be checked against a real tool result instead of the model's own unverified prior step
It disables the model's ability to guess entirely
It always uses more few-shot examples
questions.explanation: Pure CoT reasons forward from its own possibly-wrong assumptions with no way to check them. ReAct injects a real Observation after every Action, so an incorrect assumption gets corrected by actual data instead of compounding silently.

questions.prompt: What is the main practical cost of the ReAct pattern compared to a single direct answer?
options: It cannot be combined with chain-of-thought
Latency and token cost scale with the number of Thought/Action/Observation cycles
It only works with open-source models
It requires no tools at all
questions.explanation: Every hop in a ReAct trajectory is a full model round-trip plus a tool call, so a 5-hop task costs roughly 5x the latency and tokens of a single direct answer — a real tradeoff against tasks that don't need external grounding.

posts.title: Vidu S1: A Video Model You Can Talk To Live
posts.subtitle: ShengShu turns one photo into a real-time, voice-driven AI character.
posts.summary: ShengShu Technology's Vidu S1 turns a single photo into a live, voice-controlled character streaming at 25-42 FPS on a consumer GPU, using an autoregressive diffusion architecture instead of offline clip generation.

slides.title: One Photo Becomes a Live AI Character
slides.body: Unveiled July 3, 2026 at the Global Digital Economy Conference, Vidu S1 is ShengShu Technology's real-time interactive video foundation model.
bullets: Single image + live voice in
Streaming character out
Runs on a consumer GPU

slides.title: Video Models Generate Clips, Not Conversations
slides.body: Sora-class and Kling-class models denoise a whole clip in one batch job — you wait, then you get a fixed-length video back. That workflow can't hold a live conversation.
bullets: Batch generation = seconds-to-minutes of latency per clip
Fixed duration, decided before generation starts
No way to react to a voice mid-generation

slides.title: Autoregressive Diffusion, One Frame at a Time
slides.body: Instead of denoising an entire clip at once, S1 generates frame-by-frame, conditioning each new frame on the live voice stream — so the video has no fixed end and can respond as it goes.
bullets: Voice becomes a running instruction, not a one-time prompt
Drives lip sync, facial expression, gesture, and posture directly
Duration is unlimited because generation never 'closes' a clip

slides.title: The Acceleration Stack That Makes It Real-Time
slides.body: Real-time video diffusion on a consumer GPU needed a dedicated inference stack, not just a smaller model.
bullets: TurboDiffusion: fewer denoising steps per frame
Low-bit SageAttention: quantized attention kernels
Sparse attention (SLA, SpargeAttention): skip low-value attention computation

slides.title: S1 by the Numbers
Turning a Selfie Into a Livestream Host
slides.body: A streamer uploads one photo of an anime character and picks a voice. During the stream, they speak into a mic — S1 lip-syncs the character in real time, shifts expression with tone, and adds gestures, with no pre-recorded animation involved.
bullets: Same pipeline works from a photo of a real person or a pet
Use cases: interactive livestreaming, AI companionship, game NPCs, XR avatars

slides.title: Where It Falls Short
slides.body: Real-time streaming trades away fidelity that offline models keep.
bullets: 540P is well below the 1080p-4K many offline cinematic models target
Single-photo cloning of real people raises identity and consent risks
Autoregressive generation can drift over very long sessions since there's no global re-denoising pass

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: S1 reframes video generation as a live stream, not a batch job
Autoregressive diffusion + a quantized/sparse inference stack is what makes that possible on consumer hardware
It trades resolution for latency — a different point on the curve than Sora/Kling/Veo, not a strict upgrade

questions.prompt: What architectural choice lets Vidu S1 stream indefinitely instead of producing a fixed-length clip?
options: A larger transformer with more parameters
Autoregressive diffusion that generates frame-by-frame, conditioned on live input
Pre-rendering many possible endings in advance
Running multiple copies of the model in parallel
questions.explanation: Offline models denoise a whole fixed-length clip in one batch. S1 generates one frame at a time, each conditioned on the ongoing voice stream, so there's no predetermined end point.

questions.prompt: What resolution and frame rate does Vidu S1 deliver?
options: 4K at 60 FPS
1080p at 30 FPS
960×540 (540P) at 25 FPS, up to 42 FPS
720p at 15 FPS
questions.explanation: S1 targets 540P (960×540) at a 25 FPS baseline, peaking at 42 FPS — a deliberate trade of resolution for the low latency real-time interaction requires.

questions.prompt: Why does Vidu S1 need TurboDiffusion, SageAttention, and sparse attention together?
options: To increase the output resolution
To reduce per-frame compute enough to hit real-time latency on a consumer GPU
To train the model on less data
To support more languages
questions.explanation: Each technique cuts inference cost from a different angle — fewer denoising steps, quantized attention, and skipping low-value attention computation — which together bring per-frame latency low enough for live interaction on consumer hardware instead of a server cluster.

posts.title: PagedAttention: Virtual Memory for the KV Cache
posts.subtitle: How vLLM cut LLM-serving memory waste from 60-80% down to under 4%.
posts.summary: PagedAttention borrows virtual-memory paging from operating systems to store each request's KV cache in small, non-contiguous blocks, cutting GPU memory waste from 60-80% to under 4% and letting vLLM serve 2-4x more throughput than prior systems.

slides.title: Serving LLMs Wastes Most of Your GPU Memory
slides.body: Every request to an LLM needs a KV cache — and until PagedAttention, serving systems were burning 60-80% of that memory on nothing.
bullets: Introduced by the vLLM paper (UC Berkeley et al., SOSP 2023)
The core idea behind vLLM's serving engine

slides.title: Contiguous Allocation Fragments Badly
slides.body: Pre-PagedAttention systems allocated each request's KV cache as one contiguous block sized for the maximum possible sequence length — before knowing how long the output would actually be.
bullets: A request that generates 50 tokens still reserves space for, say, 2048
That reserved-but-unused space can't be given to other requests
Measured result: 60-80% of KV cache memory wasted in systems like Orca and FasterTransformer

slides.title: Borrow Paging From the OS
slides.body: PagedAttention splits each sequence's KV cache into fixed-size blocks that don't need to sit next to each other in memory — the same trick operating systems use for virtual memory.
bullets: A block table maps logical token positions to physical GPU memory blocks
New blocks are allocated on demand as generation proceeds, not all upfront
The attention kernel gathers scattered blocks through this indirection layer

slides.title: Block Tables + a Shared Physical Pool
slides.body: Instead of one long array per request, vLLM keeps a pool of fixed-size physical blocks (commonly 16 tokens each) and gives every sequence a small per-request table pointing into that shared pool.
bullets: Physical blocks are allocated from a global pool, not pre-reserved per request
Only the block table — not the KV data itself — needs to be contiguous
Freed blocks return to the pool immediately when a request finishes

slides.title: Sharing Memory Across Parallel Samples
slides.body: Say you ask a model for 4 candidate completions of the same long prompt (parallel sampling / beam search). Without PagedAttention, each of the 4 sequences duplicates the entire prompt's KV cache. With block-level copy-on-write, all 4 sequences share the same physical blocks for the prompt, and only fork a new block once a sequence's generated tokens diverge from the others.
bullets: Shared prefix = shared physical blocks, not 4 separate copies
Forking happens per-block, only where sequences actually differ

slides.title: The Measured Payoff
When the Indirection Isn't Worth It
slides.body: Paging isn't free — it trades a small amount of per-step overhead for large memory savings, and that trade doesn't always pay off.
bullets: Block-table lookups add kernel complexity vs. a flat contiguous read
With very few concurrent requests or short, uniform sequences, fragmentation was never large to begin with
Designed for inference serving — training's memory access pattern (backward pass) doesn't benefit the same way

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Contiguous KV cache allocation wastes 60-80% of memory to unused reservation headroom
PagedAttention fixes this the way OS virtual memory fixes RAM fragmentation: fixed-size blocks plus a lookup table
The memory it frees becomes more concurrent requests in flight — that's where the 2-4x throughput gain comes from
Copy-on-write block sharing is what makes beam search and parallel sampling cheap under this design

questions.prompt: What is the main source of wasted GPU memory in pre-PagedAttention LLM serving systems?
options: Model weights being stored twice
Contiguous KV cache pre-allocated for the maximum possible sequence length, mostly unused
Too many attention heads
Storing activations in FP32 instead of FP16
questions.explanation: Systems reserved one contiguous block sized for the worst-case output length per request; since most requests generate far fewer tokens, 60-80% of that reserved memory sat unused.

questions.prompt: What operating-systems concept does PagedAttention borrow?
options: Process scheduling
Virtual memory paging
File system journaling
Interrupt handling
questions.explanation: PagedAttention splits the KV cache into fixed-size, non-contiguous blocks mapped through a per-sequence block table — the same indirection trick OS virtual memory uses to avoid fragmentation.

questions.prompt: How does PagedAttention make parallel sampling (e.g., 4 completions of the same prompt) cheaper?
options: It reduces the model's parameter count
It shares physical KV cache blocks for the common prefix via copy-on-write, forking only where sequences diverge
It runs the 4 completions sequentially instead of in parallel
It skips computing attention for the shared prompt entirely
questions.explanation: Because blocks are addressed through a table rather than stored inline, multiple sequences can point at the same physical blocks for a shared prefix, only allocating new blocks once their generated tokens differ.

references.title: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention (arXiv)

posts.title: GENE-Bench: Tracing an Idea's Scientific Lineage
posts.subtitle: A benchmark that treats every paper as a genome — and tests whether models can trace how it evolved.
posts.summary: GENE-Bench ("Ideas Have Genomes") models each research paper as a genome of typed, inheritable "genes" — problem framing, method, evaluation setup — and tests whether an LLM can trace which earlier paper an idea descended from and what changed along the way. Built from 2,076 profiled papers drawn from a 29,472-paper pool, it splits into Gene-Exam (reading lineage from evidence) and Gene-Arena (generating a new idea grounded in a real lineage), evaluated across 15 systems spanning direct LLMs, research agents, and CLI harnesses.

slides.title: Papers Don't Appear From Nowhere
slides.body: Every paper inherits ideas from earlier ones, the way genes pass down traits. GENE-Bench, released July 9, 2026, turns that analogy into a testable benchmark for whether LLMs can actually trace that lineage — not just summarize a single paper.
bullets: Profiles 2,076 papers as "genome" objects, each idea, method, and claim tagged as an inheritable "gene"
Built from a pool of 29,472 papers so lineage claims can be checked against a real citation network, not synthetic data
From a Shanghai Jiao Tong University-affiliated research team

slides.title: Summarizing a Paper Isn't the Same as Explaining Its Ancestry
slides.body: Most LLM benchmarks test whether a model can read and summarize one paper. Almost none test whether it can correctly say which earlier work an idea actually descended from, and what changed on the way.
bullets: A model can paraphrase an abstract fluently while still misattributing where its core method originated
Citation lists are noisy — a paper cites dozens of works, but only a handful represent genuine intellectual inheritance
Getting this wrong matters for literature-review tools, novelty checks, and how much you can trust a research agent's claims

slides.title: GenomeDiff: Modeling What Changed Between Two Papers
slides.body: Each paper is represented as a set of typed genes. A GenomeDiff compares a paper against its ancestors — which genes were inherited unchanged, mutated, or introduced fresh.
bullets: Two arenas: Gene-Exam (42 tasks, 1,029 instances) tests reading lineage from evidence; Gene-Arena (50 tasks across 11 domains) tests generating a new idea grounded in a real lineage
Gene-Exam spans four axes: Genome Abstraction, Inheritance Tracing, Evolutionary Reasoning, and Lineage Verification
23,117 evidence spans back the golden lineage traces, so answers are checked against cited text, not just a label

slides.title: What a Lineage Task Looks Like
slides.body: A Lineage Verification item works roughly like this: the model is given a target paper's claimed method and a candidate ancestor paper, and must judge whether the method gene was truly inherited, independently invented, or misattributed.
bullets: The model can't just check if the ancestor is in the reference list — many cited papers aren't real intellectual parents
It has to point to the specific evidence span in the ancestor paper that the gene traces back to
This is the kind of check a literature-review agent would need to pass before it can be trusted to write "this builds on X"

slides.title: Who Gets Evaluated: LLMs vs. Agents vs. CLI Harnesses

rows: Single-pass model calls with no tool use
Multi-step systems that can search and cross-reference before answering
Command-line agentic setups wired to run tools during evaluation

slides.title: What GENE-Bench Doesn't Solve
slides.body: The genome framing is a useful evaluation lens, not a finished science of influence.
bullets: Lineage is graded against one curated ground-truth trace per paper — real intellectual influence is often multi-parent and fuzzier than a tree
Gene tagging requires expert-level domain judgment to build; the benchmark's ceiling is only as good as its 2,076 profiled papers
It measures whether a model can trace and generate lineage-grounded ideas — not whether the idea it invents is actually correct or novel science

slides.title: The Takeaway
slides.body: As research agents get pointed at "find related work" and "propose a new idea" tasks, benchmarks like this measure something summarization scores miss entirely.
bullets: Fluent summarization and correct attribution are different skills — GENE-Bench isolates the second one
Splitting evaluation into reading lineage (Gene-Exam) vs. generating grounded ideas (Gene-Arena) separates "can it understand" from "can it create"
Expect more literature and research-agent tools to get graded on lineage accuracy, not just answer fluency

questions.prompt: What does GENE-Bench represent each paper as, to model how ideas evolve across the literature?
options: A single embedding vector
A "genome" of typed, inheritable genes (problem framing, method, evaluation setup, etc.)
A raw citation count
A knowledge-graph triple only
questions.explanation: GENE-Bench's core idea is treating each paper as a genome made of typed genes, so lineage can be traced gene-by-gene between a paper and its ancestors via a GenomeDiff.

questions.prompt: What's the key difference between Gene-Exam and Gene-Arena?
options: Gene-Exam tests reading/tracing lineage from evidence; Gene-Arena tests generating a new lineage-grounded idea
Gene-Exam evaluates images, Gene-Arena evaluates text
They test the exact same skill at different dataset sizes
Gene-Exam is scored by humans only, Gene-Arena is fully automatic
questions.explanation: Gene-Exam (42 tasks, 1,029 instances) checks whether a model can correctly read and trace lineage from evidence, while Gene-Arena (50 tasks across 11 domains) checks whether it can generate a new idea that's genuinely grounded in a real lineage.

questions.prompt: Per the benchmark's motivation, why isn't a paper's citation list enough to establish lineage?
options: Citations are only available in PDF format
A paper typically cites dozens of works, but only a few represent genuine intellectual inheritance
Most papers don't include citations
LLMs are physically unable to parse reference lists
questions.explanation: Citation lists are noisy — most cited works are context or comparisons, not true ancestors of an idea — so GENE-Bench requires evidence-backed lineage claims instead of just checking reference-list membership.

references.title: Ideas Have Genomes: Benchmarking Scientific Lineage Reasoning and Evolutionary Idea Generation (arXiv:2607.08758)

posts.title: Claude Sonnet 5: Anthropic's Cheaper Agentic Workhorse

It's priced well under Opus 4.8 ($2/$10 per million input/output tokens through August 31, 2026, then $3/$15), while reportedly closing much of the agentic-capability gap — including beating Opus 4.8 on Terminal-Bench 2.1, even as Opus retains a lead on SWE-bench Pro.

posts.title: Attention Sinks: Why the First Tokens Never Get Evicted
posts.subtitle: A fixed KV cache breaks the moment you evict the wrong tokens — unless you know which ones to protect.
posts.summary: StreamingLLM (Xiao et al., 2023) found that naively evicting old tokens from a fixed-size KV cache causes a catastrophic perplexity spike the moment the first few tokens leave — because softmax attention needs somewhere to dump unneeded weight, and early tokens become that default sink. Pinning just 4 sink tokens alongside a normal sliding window restores stable perplexity on sequences up to millions of tokens, with no retraining required — though it buys streaming stability, not genuine long-range recall.

slides.title: Long Conversations Break Fixed-Size Caches
slides.body: Streaming a model through a very long or open-ended conversation with a fixed-size KV cache means evicting old tokens — and naive eviction quietly destroys the model.
bullets: A transformer's KV cache grows by one entry per token; left unbounded it eventually exhausts GPU memory
The obvious fix — keep only the most recent N tokens ("sliding window") — seems safe since recent context matters most
StreamingLLM (Xiao et al., 2023) found that fix catastrophically breaks the model the moment the very first tokens leave the cache

slides.title: Window Attention Quietly Explodes
slides.body: The failure isn't gradual degradation — it's a cliff.
bullets: Once the initial 3-4 tokens are evicted from a sliding window, perplexity spikes by orders of magnitude and the model effectively stops working
This happens even when those first tokens are semantically meaningless — a BOS token, or the start of an unrelated sentence
That cliff ruled out naive windowing for any long-running streaming deployment, long before anyone understood why it happened

slides.title: Attention Sinks: Softmax Needs Somewhere to Dump Weight
slides.body: Xiao et al. traced the cliff to how softmax attention works: at every layer, attention scores across all visible keys must sum to 1, even when a token has nothing relevant to attend to.
bullets: Because early tokens are visible to every later token during autoregressive training, they become the model's default "no-op" target for unneeded attention mass
These first few tokens absorb a disproportionate share of attention regardless of their actual content — an "attention sink"
Evict them and softmax normalization has nowhere safe left to send that leftover weight, corrupting every downstream attention distribution

slides.title: The Fix: 4 Sink Tokens Plus a Sliding Window
slides.body: StreamingLLM keeps a tiny fixed set of initial tokens permanently cached alongside a normal sliding window of recent tokens — no retraining required.
bullets: As few as 4 sink tokens combined with a recent-token window restores stable perplexity indefinitely
Tested stable on sequences up to 4 million tokens with a constant-size cache
Purely an inference-time cache-management trick — the model's weights and training are untouched

slides.title: A Support Bot That Never Restarts
slides.body: Consider a customer-support bot with an 8K-token cache budget, running a conversation that grows past 100K tokens over a long session.
bullets: Naive windowing: once past ~8K tokens, evicting the earliest turns (including the sink) tanks output quality — the bot starts producing garbled or repetitive text
StreamingLLM: 4 sink tokens (e.g., the system prompt's opening tokens) stay pinned while everything else slides — quality stays stable through hour-long sessions
Memory footprint stays flat at the cache budget the whole time, unlike approaches that either recompute the full history or silently degrade

slides.title: Dense vs. Window-Only vs. Attention Sinks

rows: Explodes once sink tokens are evicted
Stable up to millions of tokens

slides.title: What Attention Sinks Don't Give You
slides.body: This is a stability fix, not a memory upgrade.
bullets: It buys streaming stability, not memory — the model still can't recall specific facts from turns pushed outside the window
Sink tokens are chosen by position (the first few), not by content — it's a structural fix, not a relevance-based one
For tasks that need genuine long-range recall, pair this with retrieval or a dedicated long-context architecture instead of relying on sinks alone

slides.title: The Takeaway
slides.body: Attention sinks explain a real, measurable failure mode of naive KV-cache eviction — and the fix is nearly free.
bullets: Pin a handful of initial tokens, slide the rest — no fine-tuning needed to keep a streaming model stable indefinitely
It's a streaming-stability tool, not a context-extension tool — know the difference before reaching for it
Most modern long-context serving stacks now bake this in by default, precisely because the failure mode it prevents is so severe

questions.prompt: What happens to a transformer's output quality when a sliding-window cache evicts the very first tokens of a sequence?
options: Nothing changes — recent tokens matter more anyway
Perplexity spikes dramatically and the model effectively breaks
The model runs faster with no quality change
Output becomes more accurate since noise is removed
questions.explanation: StreamingLLM found this failure is a cliff, not a gradual decline: evicting the first few tokens causes perplexity to spike by orders of magnitude.

questions.prompt: Why do the first few tokens absorb so much attention regardless of their actual content?
options: They are always the highest-frequency tokens in the vocabulary
Softmax attention scores must sum to 1, and early tokens — visible to every later token — become the default target for unneeded attention mass
The model was explicitly fine-tuned to prioritize them
They contain the most semantic information in the sequence
questions.explanation: Because softmax normalization forces attention weights to sum to 1 even when nothing is relevant, and early tokens are visible to all later tokens during training, they become a structural "sink" for leftover attention mass.

questions.prompt: What does StreamingLLM's attention-sink fix actually solve?
options: It extends the model's true long-range memory and recall
It stabilizes streaming inference with a fixed-size cache, without adding true long-range recall
It requires retraining the model from scratch on longer sequences
It only works on sequences shorter than 1,000 tokens
questions.explanation: Attention sinks keep perplexity stable during long streaming inference with a fixed cache — they don't give the model the ability to recall specific facts from evicted turns.

posts.title: LoRA: Fine-Tuning a 70B Model by Training 0.1% of It
posts.subtitle: Freeze the base weights, learn two skinny matrices, and merge them back in for free at inference.
posts.summary: LoRA freezes a pretrained model's weights and injects a trainable low-rank update (two small matrices, B and A) into each target layer instead of updating every parameter. The original paper reports up to a 10,000x cut in trainable parameters and 3x less GPU memory versus full fine-tuning GPT-3 175B — and because the update can be merged back into the base weights, it adds zero inference latency. QLoRA extends the trick to 4-bit-quantized base models, making 65B-class fine-tuning possible on a single GPU.

slides.title: Full Fine-Tuning Doesn't Scale
slides.body: Fine-tuning every parameter of a large model means storing a full second copy of the weights plus Adam's momentum and variance terms — often 3-4x the model's own size in optimizer state alone.
bullets: A 70B model in fp16 is ~140GB of weights before you even add gradients or optimizer state
Every downstream task traditionally meant its own full-size checkpoint
LoRA asks: what if we froze the base model and only trained a tiny update?

slides.title: Earlier Fixes Were Slow or Shallow
slides.body: Before LoRA, the two dominant lightweight fine-tuning tricks each traded away something important.
bullets: Adapter layers (Houlsby et al., 2019) insert small bottleneck layers into the forward pass — cheap to train, but they add sequential compute, so every inference call gets slower
Prompt/prefix tuning trains a soft prompt prepended to the input — near-zero parameters, but it eats into your context window and often underperforms on smaller base models
Neither approach lets you get back to a model that's architecturally identical to the original at inference time

slides.title: Freeze the Weights, Learn a Low-Rank Update
slides.body: LoRA (Hu et al., 2021) freezes the pretrained weight matrix W0 and represents the fine-tuning update as a product of two small matrices: ΔW = B · A.
bullets: W0 is d×k; B is d×r and A is r×k, where the rank r (commonly 4-64) is far smaller than d or k
Only B and A are trained — W0 never receives a gradient update
Applied mainly to the attention projection matrices (query and value); the paper's GPT-3 experiments needed as little as r=1 or r=2 on some tasks to match full fine-tuning

slides.title: The Merge Trick: Zero Extra Inference Cost
slides.body: Because ΔW = B·A has the exact same shape as W0, you can just add them together once training is done.
bullets: W' = W0 + B·A — a single matrix addition, computed once, folded back into the base model
The served model has the identical architecture and identical latency as the original — unlike adapter layers, LoRA adds nothing to the forward pass
Keep B and A separate instead of merging, and you can hot-swap tiny per-task adapters (often tens of MB) against one shared frozen base — the trick modern multi-tenant LoRA-serving systems rely on

slides.title: Worked Example: A 7B Support-Bot Fine-Tune
slides.body: Turning a general 7B chat model into a customer-support specialist.
bullets: Full fine-tuning: update all ~7B params, store fp32 Adam momentum + variance for each — memory footprint balloons to several times the base weights, usually forcing multi-GPU training
LoRA at rank 16 on the query/value projections across every transformer block: trainable parameters land around a few million — well under 1% of the 7B total
Result: training fits on a single high-memory consumer GPU, and the saved adapter is tens of MB instead of the ~14GB fp16 checkpoint a full fine-tune would produce

slides.title: LoRA vs. the Alternatives

rows: Best ceiling, most memory/compute to train
Cheap to train, slower to serve
Sweet spot: cheap to train, free to serve
Same as LoRA, on a 4-bit frozen base

slides.title: Tradeoffs and When Not to Use It
slides.body: LoRA is not a free lunch — it trades ceiling for efficiency.
bullets: Too low a rank underfits tasks that need substantial new knowledge (a new language, a large factual domain) — those often still need full fine-tuning or a larger rank
LoRA only reduces training cost, not inference compute — the merged model runs at exactly the same FLOPs as the dense base model
QLoRA's 4-bit base adds a small quantization-error floor and dequantization overhead during training versus full 16-bit LoRA — the tradeoff for fitting a much bigger model on one GPU
It can't add new vocabulary or architectural capacity — if the task needs a bigger tokenizer or new layers, LoRA can't help

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: LoRA freezes the pretrained weights and learns a low-rank update ΔW = B·A instead of updating every parameter
The original paper reports up to 10,000x fewer trainable parameters and 3x less GPU memory versus full fine-tuning GPT-3 175B
Because ΔW merges back into the base weight matrix, LoRA adds zero inference latency — unlike adapter layers
QLoRA combines LoRA with a 4-bit quantized frozen base, making it possible to fine-tune 65B-class models on a single GPU

questions.prompt: What does LoRA actually train?
options: All of the pretrained model's weights, at a lower learning rate
Two small matrices (B and A) whose product approximates the fine-tuning update, while the base weights stay frozen
A new tokenizer and embedding table
A separate reward model used only at inference time
questions.explanation: LoRA freezes the pretrained weight matrix W0 entirely and represents the task-specific update as ΔW = B·A, where B and A are much smaller than W0. Only B and A receive gradient updates.

questions.prompt: Why doesn't a merged LoRA adapter add any inference latency, unlike bottleneck adapter layers?
options: Because LoRA runs on a separate, faster chip
Because ΔW = B·A has the same shape as W0, so it can be added directly into the base weight matrix before serving — the forward pass is architecturally unchanged
Because LoRA skips the attention layers entirely
Because LoRA reduces the model's total parameter count
questions.explanation: B·A produces a matrix the exact same shape as the original W0, so W0 + B·A can be computed once and folded back in. The served model has identical architecture and cost to the original — unlike adapter layers, which insert new layers into the sequential forward pass and add latency.

questions.prompt: What does QLoRA add on top of standard LoRA?
options: A larger rank r for every layer
A second, larger LoRA adapter for redundancy
4-bit quantization of the frozen base model, so far larger models fit on a single GPU during fine-tuning
Removal of the attention mechanism
questions.explanation: QLoRA keeps the LoRA idea (frozen base + trainable low-rank update) but quantizes the frozen base weights to 4-bit precision, dramatically cutting the memory needed to hold the base model during training — enough to fine-tune 65B-class models on one 48GB GPU.

references.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs

posts.title: Gemma 4: One Model Family, No Separate Encoders
posts.subtitle: Google DeepMind's new open-weight line drops the vision/audio encoder entirely for its 12B model — and still tops the open leaderboard.
posts.summary: Gemma 4 is Google DeepMind's new open-weight family (2.3B to 31B params, dense and MoE) that adds a 'thinking mode' and, for its 12B model, replaces separate vision/audio encoders with a unified pipeline that ingests raw pixels and audio directly. The 31B Dense variant leads open-weight models on MMLU-Pro, AIME 2026, and LiveCodeBench v6.

slides.title: Four Sizes, One Encoder-Free Idea
slides.body: Google DeepMind's Gemma 4 Technical Report (published July 2, 2026) introduces a family that spans 2.3B to 31B parameters — and for the first time, one of its models skips dedicated vision and audio encoders altogether.
bullets: 2.3B → 31B params, dense + Mixture-of-Experts
31B Dense: 85.2% MMLU-Pro, tops open-weight models
New: encoder-free multimodal ingestion + "thinking mode"

slides.title: Multimodal Models Are Bolted Together
slides.body: Most vision-language models are really three models stitched into one: a vision transformer, an audio encoder, and a language model, each pretrained separately then glued together with a projector.
bullets: Separate encoders mean separate pretraining objectives that may not align with the language model
Extra encoder forward passes add latency and memory — expensive on small, on-device models
Every new modality historically meant bolting on another dedicated encoder

slides.title: The Family: Dense and MoE, Interleaved Attention
slides.body: The Gemma 4 suite ships four variants, all using hybrid attention that interleaves local sliding-window layers with full global layers (the final layer is always global).
bullets: E2B: 2.3B effective params · E4B: 4.5B effective params — both 128K context
26B A4B (MoE): 25.2B total params, only 3.8B active per token
31B Dense: 30.7B params, 256K context window

slides.title: The 12B Model Skips the Encoder Entirely
slides.body: For its 12B model specifically, the paper proposes 'a unified, encoder-free architecture... which ingests raw audio and image patches' — no separate ViT, no conformer audio stack.
bullets: Vision: raw 48×48 pixel patches → projected into the LLM hidden dimension with one matrix multiply; spatial position comes from factorized coordinate lookup tables
Audio: raw 16kHz audio sliced into 40ms frames → linearly projected straight into the LLM input space
Other sizes keep improved (but still separate) vision and audio encoders

slides.title: Thinking Mode Joins the Family
slides.body: Alongside the architecture change, Gemma 4 integrates a thinking mode across the family — the model generates a reasoning trace before producing its final answer, rather than only being able to answer directly.
bullets: Toggleable per-request rather than a separate model checkpoint
Mirrors the reasoning-trace pattern already standard in frontier closed models
Bundled with the same weights — no separate "reasoning" SKU to download

slides.title: Worked Example: A Photo, No ViT Required
slides.body: Take a 480×480 pixel image fed to the 12B model.
bullets: Sliced into 48×48 patches → a 10×10 grid = 100 patches total
Each patch is flattened and passed through a single matrix multiply straight into the transformer's hidden dimension
A factorized coordinate lookup adds "this patch is at row 3, col 7" — no separate vision transformer forward pass ever runs
Net effect: one fewer full sub-model to load and run before the language model even starts reasoning about the image

slides.title: Where It Lands on the Leaderboard — and Where It Might Not
slides.body: The 31B Dense model leads open-weight models on several benchmarks; the encoder-free design is unproven at every size.
bullets: 31B Dense: 85.2% MMLU-Pro · 89.2% AIME 2026 · 80.0% LiveCodeBench v6 · 76.9% MMMU Pro
26B MoE: 82.6% MMLU-Pro while activating just 3.8B params per token

Encoder-free ingestion loses the contrastive image-text pretraining (à la CLIP) that dedicated vision encoders get — it may trail specialist encoders on fine-grained visual tasks the paper doesn't benchmark in depth
Only the 12B size ships encoder-free today — E2B/E4B and the 31B Dense model still use separate encoders

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: Gemma 4 spans 2.3B–31B params across dense and MoE variants, all open-weight
The 12B model is the first in the family to ingest raw pixels and raw audio with no dedicated encoder
A shared "thinking mode" adds reasoning traces without a separate model checkpoint
31B Dense tops open-weight benchmarks, but encoder-free is a one-size bet, not yet the whole family's default

questions.prompt: What makes the Gemma 4 12B model architecturally different from the other sizes in the family?
options: It uses a much larger vision transformer
It ingests raw image patches and raw audio frames with no dedicated encoder
It is the only variant with a 256K context window
It is the only dense (non-MoE) variant
questions.explanation: Every other Gemma 4 size keeps improved but separate vision and audio encoders. Only the 12B model uses the unified, encoder-free pipeline that projects raw 48×48 pixel patches and raw 40ms audio frames directly into the language model.

questions.prompt: The 26B A4B Mixture-of-Experts model has 25.2B total parameters. How many are active per token?
questions.explanation: MoE models route each token through only a subset of experts. The 26B A4B variant activates just 3.8B of its 25.2B total parameters per token — most of the capacity stays idle for any given token, which is exactly how MoE keeps inference cheap at large total scale.

questions.prompt: What is the main tradeoff of the encoder-free approach used in Gemma 4 12B?
options: It requires far more training data than encoder-based models
It cannot process audio at all
It loses the modality-specific contrastive pretraining that dedicated encoders (like CLIP-style vision encoders) provide
It only works with black-and-white images
questions.explanation: Dedicated vision/audio encoders are usually pretrained with objectives (like image-text contrastive learning) tuned specifically for that modality. Skipping the encoder trades that specialist pretraining for simplicity and speed — a real tradeoff, not a free win.

references.title: Gemma 4 Technical Report (arXiv:2607.02770)

posts.subtitle: Built on Codex and GPT-5.6, it plans, reaches into your apps, and hands back finished documents — not just drafts.

slides.title: OpenAI's Next Move: An Agent for the Whole Job
slides.body: On July 9, 2026, OpenAI launched ChatGPT Work — pitched not as a chatbot that drafts, but an agent that finishes.
bullets: Built on Codex, powered by GPT-5.6
Connects to Gmail, Slack, Google, Salesforce, HubSpot
Can work independently on a task "for hours"

slides.title: Chat Assistants Stop at the Draft
slides.body: A standard chatbot can write you a paragraph or a formula, but someone still has to open the spreadsheet, paste it in, check the CRM, and stitch the pieces together.
bullets: Multi-step office work spans multiple apps a chat window can't reach into
Long projects need the assistant to persist across many steps, not answer once and stop
Previous "agent" demos often broke on real, messy, multi-app workflows

slides.title: What ChatGPT Work Actually Does
slides.body: It gathers context, plans an approach, and takes action across connected tools and files to produce finished output.
bullets: Connects to Gmail, Slack, Google, and CRM tools like Salesforce and HubSpot to pull emails, docs, chats, and customer data
Breaks a complex goal into smaller steps and works through them independently, staying with a project for hours if needed
Outputs finished materials directly — spreadsheets, docs, slides, and web apps — not just text replies

Cross-references recent Gmail threads for context on deals in flight

Returns a finished slide deck — without you re-uploading a single file or copy-pasting between tabs

slides.body: Per 1M tokens, short-context rates:

rows: Everyday work, GPT-5.5-class at lower cost

slides.title: Where the Risk Lives
slides.body: Giving an agent write access to Gmail, Slack, and your CRM is a bigger blast radius than a chat reply.
bullets: A single wrong action across connected accounts (a bad email send, a wrong CRM update) is riskier than a bad chat answer — permissioning and review matter more here
Rollout is staged: desktop app available today across plans, but Plus and Business users on web/mobile wait "the next few days"
Launch lands the same week Anthropic expanded Claude's own agentic Cowork features — office-agent competition is intensifying, not settling

slides.title: Takeaways
bullets: ChatGPT Work = Codex + GPT-5.6, aimed at finishing multi-step office work, not just drafting it
It connects directly to Gmail, Slack, Google, and CRM tools to gather context and take action
GPT-5.6 ships in three priced tiers — Sol, Terra, Luna — trading reasoning depth for cost and speed
Broader app access means bigger consequences for mistakes — treat early rollout with the same caution as any new write-access integration

It can take real actions across connected apps like Gmail, Slack, and CRM tools, so a mistake has a bigger blast radius
It cannot be used on mobile devices
