posts.title: State Space Models: Attention's Linear-Time Rival
posts.subtitle: Mamba compresses an entire sequence into one fixed-size state, trading a little exact recall for constant-memory inference at any context length.
posts.summary: State Space Models (SSMs) like Mamba process sequences in linear time by compressing history into a fixed-size hidden state instead of attending over every past token. Mamba's selective mechanism makes that compression input-dependent — the model chooses what to remember per token — and a hardware-aware parallel scan keeps training fast on GPUs, at the cost of weaker exact long-range recall than full attention.

slides.title: What If a Model Never Had to Look Back?
slides.body: Self-attention re-reads every previous token at every step. State space models instead carry one running summary forward — like a hidden state in an RNN, but engineered to train in parallel and remember selectively.
bullets: Core promise: process a sequence in O(n) time instead of attention's O(n²)
Popularized by Mamba (Gu & Dao) and used in hybrids like Jamba and Nemotron-H
Inference memory stays constant per step — no growing KV cache

slides.title: Attention's Quadratic Bill Comes Due at Long Context
slides.body: Every token in a Transformer attends to every other token, so both compute and the KV cache grow with context length — quadratically for compute, linearly per token for cache memory that keeps accumulating.
bullets: A 200K-token context means a KV cache that keeps growing the whole conversation
Classic RNNs are O(n) and constant-memory, but train sequentially and forget over long ranges
The gap SSMs try to close: RNN-like efficiency without RNN-like training and memory problems

slides.title: Selective State: Choosing What to Remember, Per Token
slides.body: Earlier SSMs (like S4) had fixed, input-independent dynamics — the same decay/update rule regardless of content. Mamba's key change is making those dynamics selective: the update depends on the current token, so the model can let irrelevant tokens pass through unchanged and let important ones overwrite the state.
bullets: S4-style SSMs: linear time-invariant — same update rule every step
Mamba: input-dependent (selective) update — content decides what's kept or dropped
This selectivity is what let SSMs finally compete with attention on language modeling quality

slides.title: Trains Like a Scan, Runs Inference Like an RNN
slides.body: The recurrence looks sequential, but a hardware-aware parallel scan (plus kernel fusion that avoids materializing large intermediates in GPU memory) lets Mamba train in parallel across the sequence, closing much of the throughput gap with attention.
bullets: Training: parallel scan algorithm processes the whole sequence at once on GPU
Inference: recurrent mode — one fixed-size state updated per new token
No KV cache to grow: memory per generated token stays flat regardless of context length

slides.title: Attention vs Selective State Space, Side by Side
slides.body: Neither is strictly better — they trade compute pattern for recall precision.

slides.title: Streaming a 500K-Token Sensor Log Without the Memory Blowup
slides.body: An agent ingesting a continuous 500K-token log stream with a Transformer would carry a KV cache that keeps growing for the whole session. A Mamba-style layer instead keeps one fixed-size hidden state updated per token — memory per step never grows, which is why production hybrids like Jamba (AI21) and Nvidia's Nemotron-H interleave Mamba layers with a handful of attention layers for exactly this workload.
bullets: Jamba: alternates Mamba and attention blocks in one model
Nemotron-H: mostly Mamba-2 layers with sparse attention layers mixed in
Goal of hybrids: keep SSM's flat memory, borrow attention's precise recall where it matters most

slides.title: When to Reach for an SSM — and When Not To
slides.body: SSMs win on throughput and memory at long context; attention still wins when you need to pinpoint one exact fact from far back.
bullets: Use SSM/hybrid layers for: very long or streaming context, cheap constant-memory inference
Stick with full attention for: short-context tasks needing precise 'needle in a haystack' recall
Pure-SSM models tend to underperform on exact associative recall — most production systems hybridize
Not a drop-in replacement everywhere: pick per workload, not by default

questions.prompt: What is Mamba's key innovation over earlier state space models like S4?
options: It removes the hidden state entirely
It makes the state-transition dynamics input-dependent (selective) instead of fixed
It adds more attention heads
It only works on images
questions.explanation: S4-style SSMs use the same linear time-invariant update at every step regardless of content. Mamba makes that update depend on the current input, so the model can selectively remember or forget per token.

questions.prompt: Why does per-token inference memory stay flat for an SSM but keep growing for attention?
options: SSMs don't process all tokens
Attention needs a KV cache that grows with every new token in context, while an SSM updates one fixed-size state
Attention uses less memory in general
SSMs store the entire input as raw text
questions.explanation: Attention must keep every past token's key/value vectors around to attend to them later, so the KV cache grows with context length. An SSM instead compresses all history into one fixed-size hidden state that gets updated, not extended.

questions.prompt: Why do production models like Jamba and Nemotron-H mix Mamba layers with a few attention layers instead of using pure SSM?
options: To make the model slower on purpose
Because pure SSMs can't run on GPUs
To recover attention's precise long-range recall while keeping most of SSM's memory and speed advantage
Attention layers are required by law for AI safety
questions.explanation: Pure SSMs compress history into a fixed state and can underperform on tasks needing exact recall of a specific far-back token. Hybrids interleave a handful of attention layers to patch that weakness while keeping most of the SSM efficiency.

references.title: Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces (arXiv:2312.00752)

posts.title: SkillOpt: Training the Skill Doc, Not the Model
posts.subtitle: Microsoft Research's text-space optimizer edits a single skill file until it wins on 52 out of 52 benchmark cells — with zero extra inference cost.
posts.summary: SkillOpt (arXiv:2605.23904, Microsoft Research with SJTU/Tongji/Fudan) treats an agent's natural-language skill document as the thing you optimize, not the model weights: a separate optimizer model proposes bounded edits from scored rollouts, and an edit only survives if it strictly improves a held-out validation score. Across 6 benchmarks, 7 target models, and 3 execution harnesses, it was best-or-tied on all 52 cells, lifting GPT-5.5's average accuracy by up to +24.8 points with zero added inference-time cost.

slides.title: What If the Skill File Were the Thing You Trained?
slides.body: Posted to arXiv in May 2026 (arXiv:2605.23904), SkillOpt comes from Microsoft Research with SJTU, Tongji, and Fudan. It treats a frozen agent's natural-language skill document as external, trainable state — optimized with the same discipline as weight-space training, but without touching a single weight.
bullets: arXiv:2605.23904 — 'SkillOpt: Executive Strategy for Self-Evolving Agent Skills'
Core idea: a separate optimizer model edits a skill markdown file based on scored rollouts
Headline result: best-or-tied on all 52 of 52 (model, benchmark, harness) cells tested

slides.title: Today's Agent Skills Are Written Once and Hoped For
slides.body: Agent 'skills' — the instructions/playbooks an agent reads before acting — are usually hand-written, generated once by an LLM, or loosely self-revised. None of that behaves like a real optimizer: there's no guarantee a revision actually helps, and no principled way to know when to stop.
bullets: Hand-crafted skills don't adapt to where the agent is actually failing
One-shot LLM-generated skills are never checked against a validation signal
Loose self-revision can drift the skill worse over time with no accept/reject gate

slides.title: Bounded Edits, Accepted Only If They Beat a Held-Out Score
slides.body: SkillOpt runs the agent, scores the rollouts, and has a separate optimizer model propose bounded add/delete/replace edits to a single skill document. An edit is kept only when it strictly improves a held-out validation score — otherwise it's discarded.
bullets: Optimizer model turns scored rollouts into small, bounded edits — not full rewrites
Validation-gated updates: an edit ships only if it strictly beats the current held-out score
Zero inference-time overhead — the trained artifact is just a markdown file the frozen agent reads

slides.title: Three Mechanisms That Keep Text Optimization Stable
slides.body: Editing a text document by gradient-like feedback can easily thrash. SkillOpt borrows stability tricks from weight-space optimization and reimplements them in text.
bullets: A textual 'learning-rate' budget caps how much a skill document can change per round
A rejected-edit buffer remembers edits that failed validation so they aren't retried
Epoch-wise slow/meta updates smooth out noisy single-rollout signal before committing a change

slides.title: Best-or-Tied on Every Cell, Against Six Prior Approaches
slides.body: The paper evaluates 6 benchmarks (including SpreadsheetBench, OfficeQA, and LiveMathematicianBench) across 7 target models and 3 execution harnesses (direct chat, Codex, Claude Code) — 52 total cells.

slides.title: The Numbers, and a Skill That Moved Between Tools
slides.body: On GPT-5.5 in direct chat, procedural tasks saw the largest jumps: SpreadsheetBench rose from 41.8 to 80.7, OfficeQA from 33.1 to 72.1, LiveMathematicianBench from 37.6 to 66.9. Averaged across benchmarks, SkillOpt lifted GPT-5.5's accuracy by +23.5 points in direct chat, +24.8 inside the Codex agentic loop, and +19.1 inside Claude Code.
bullets: SpreadsheetBench: 41.8 → 80.7 (GPT-5.5, direct chat, no-skill vs. SkillOpt-trained skill)
A spreadsheet skill trained entirely inside the Codex loop was dropped into Claude Code unchanged
That transferred skill drove a +59.7 point gain over Claude Code's own native baseline

slides.title: Where Text-Space Optimization Runs Out of Road
slides.body: SkillOpt trains the instructions, not the model — which is exactly its limit as well as its strength.
bullets: It needs a scorable validation signal for every task; skills for hard-to-grade, open-ended work have less to optimize against
A single bounded skill document can't fix a base model that's fundamentally incapable of a task
Training still costs optimizer-model calls and rollouts, even though deployment is free of overhead
Bounded add/delete/replace edits favor incremental improvement over rethinking a skill from scratch

slides.title: Takeaways
slides.body: SkillOpt reframes 'prompt engineering for agents' as a real optimization problem: score it, edit it, validate it, keep only what wins.
bullets: Skills become external, trainable state for a frozen agent — no weight updates, no extra inference cost
Validation-gated edits (not free-form self-revision) are what make the process reliable enough to beat 6 prior methods
Best-or-tied on 52/52 tested cells, with gains up to +24.8 points and skills that transfer across execution harnesses
The tradeoff: it needs a scorable task and rollouts to learn from — it isn't magic for ungraded, wide-open work

questions.prompt: What does SkillOpt actually optimize?
options: The agent model's weights via fine-tuning
A natural-language skill document, treated as the agent's external trainable state
The tokenizer vocabulary
A separate reward model used for RLHF
questions.explanation: SkillOpt keeps the agent frozen and instead edits a skill markdown file — treating that document as the thing to train, with zero added inference-time cost at deployment.

questions.prompt: How does SkillOpt decide whether to keep a proposed edit to the skill document?
options: It always accepts the optimizer model's suggested edit
It picks the edit with the most words changed
It keeps the edit only if it strictly improves a held-out validation score
It asks a human reviewer for every edit
questions.explanation: Validation-gated updates are central to SkillOpt: an edit ships only when it strictly beats the current held-out score, which is what keeps the optimization from drifting or thrashing.

questions.prompt: What happened when a skill trained inside the Codex harness was moved into Claude Code unchanged?
options: It failed completely and had to be retrained
It drove a +59.7 point gain over Claude Code's native baseline
Performance was identical to no skill at all
It only worked after converting it to a different file format
questions.explanation: The transferred spreadsheet skill produced a +59.7 point gain over Claude Code's own baseline with no further changes — evidence that a trained skill document can generalize across execution harnesses, not just across prompts.

references.title: SkillOpt: Executive Strategy for Self-Evolving Agent Skills (arXiv:2605.23904)
